name: HMS Upright (N 89)
events: The history of HMS Upright as compiled on this page is extracted from the patrol reports and logbooks of this submarine and was created in April 2013. Corrections and details regarding information from the enemy's side (for instance the composition of convoys attacked) is kindly provided by Mr. Platon Alexiades, a naval researcher from Canada.
This page dealing with the wartime history of HMS Upright was compiled in April 2013.
This page was last updated in February 2026.

3 Sep 1940 Upright (Lt. F.J. Brooks, RN) conducted acceptance trials off Barrow. These were completed successfully and the submarine was excepted from the shipyard subject that she would return to Barrow to change propellers for which she immediately returned to Barrow.

During the trials she was escorted by HMS Eskimo (Cdr. St. J.A. Micklethwait, DSO and Bar, RN) which had just completed battle damage repairs at Barrow and was also conducting post repair trials herself. (1)
4 Sep 1940 HMS Upright (Lt. F.J. Brooks, RN) is docked at Barrow to change the propellers. (1)
5 Sep 1940 HMS Upright (Lt. F.J. Brooks, RN) is undocked and departed Barrow around 1230 A/5 for Rothesay. She does not appeared to have been escorted. (1)
6 Sep 1940 Around 0715 A/6, HMS Upright (Lt. F.J. Brooks, RN) arrived at Rothesay for a period of training.
7 Sep 1940 HMS Upright (Lt. F.J. Brooks, RN) conducted exercises in the Clyde area while she was escorted by HMS Breda (Capt.(Retd.) A.E. Johnston, RN). (1)
8 Sep 1940 HMS Upright (Lt. F.J. Brooks, RN) conducted noise trials in Loch Striven and later in Kames Bay. (1)
10 Sep 1940 HMS Upright (Lt. F.J. Brooks, RN) conducted exercises in the Clyde area. (1)
11 Sep 1940 HMS Upright (Lt. F.J. Brooks, RN) conducted attack exercises in the Clyde area which HMS Breda (Capt.(Retd.) A.E. Johnston, RN) acted as the target. (1)
12 Sep 1940 HMS Otway (Lt. E.D. Norman, RN), HMS Upright (Lt. F.J. Brooks, RN) and HMS Utmost (Lt. J.H. Eaden, DSC, RN) all conducted exercises in the Clyde area. (2)
13 Sep 1940 HMS Upright (Lt. F.J. Brooks, RN) conducted degaussing trials off Helensburgh. (1)
15 Sep 1940 HMS Upright (Lt. F.J. Brooks, RN) proceeded from Holy Loch to Arrochar where torpedo discharge trials were to be carried out on the torpedo firing range. (1)
16 Sep 1940 HMS Upright (Lt. F.J. Brooks, RN) conducted torpedo discharge trials off Arrochar. (1)
17 Sep 1940 HMS Upright (Lt. F.J. Brooks, RN) conducted torpedo discharge trials off Arrochar. (1)
18 Sep 1940 HMS Upright (Lt. F.J. Brooks, RN) conducted torpedo discharge trials off Arrochar. (1)
19 Sep 1940 HMS Upright (Lt. F.J. Brooks, RN) conducted torpedo discharge trials off Arrochar. (1)
20 Sep 1940 HMS Upright (Lt. F.J. Brooks, RN) proceeded from Arrochar to Holy Loch but before proceeding to Holy Loch speed trials were carried out on the measured mile in Loch Long.
22 Sep 1940 HMS Upright (Lt. F.J. Brooks, RN) conducted degaussing trials at Gare Loch. (1)
23 Sep 1940 HMS Upright (Lt. F.J. Brooks, RN) conducted attack exercises in the Clyde area with HMS Breda (Capt.(Retd.) A.E. Johnston, RN) serving as the target. These included night exercises. (1)
24 Sep 1940 HMS Upright (Lt. F.J. Brooks, RN) returned to Rothesay upon completion of the night attack exercises. (1)
26 Sep 1940 Around 2000 A/26, HMS Upright (Lt. F.J. Brooks, RN) departed Rothesay for Portsmouth. She was escorted by HMS Surprise (Capt.(Retd.) E. Stubbs, RN).
For the daily positions of HMS Upright during this passage see the map below.
30 Sep 1940 Around 1600 A/30, HMS Upright (Lt. F.J. Brooks, RN) arrived at Portsmouth (Gosport, Haslar Creek, HMS Dolphin).
Before arrival noise trials and D/G trials were carried out. (1)
4 Oct 1940 Around 1550 A/4, HMS Upright (Lt. F.J. Brooks, RN) departed Portsmouth for her 1st war patrol. She was ordered to patrol in the English Channel.
She was escorted by HMS Hugh Walpole (Skr. D. Upson, RNR).
Earlier in the day, before departure independent exercises had been carried out.
At 2204 A/4, HMS Utmost arrived in the waiting position 50°10'N, 01°25'W. (3)
4 Oct 1940 For the daily positions of HMS Upright during her 1st war patrol see the map below.
5 Oct 1940 At 0935 A/5, HMS Upright (Lt. F.J. Brooks, RN) received orders to proceed to area 'W 2' and patrol there.
At 1702 A/5, orders were received to exchange patrol areas with HMS Ursula (Lt.Cdr. W.A.K.N. Cavaye, RN). (3)
6 Oct 1940 At 0600 A/6, HMS Upright (Lt. F.J. Brooks, RN) entered patrol area 'D 4' to patrol there 5 to 7 miles from the coast. (3)

7 Oct 1940 At 2003 A/7, the Capt. S.5's signal timed 1850 A/7 was received which informed HMS Upright (Lt. F.J. Brooks, RN) that enemy motor vessels coming from Boulogne were expected to pass through her patrol area this night. (4)

8 Oct 1940 At 1630 B/8, in position 49°53'N, 00°32'E, HMS Upright (Lt. F.J. Brooks, RN) closed the pillar buoy reported by HMS L 27 (Lt. R.E. Campbell, RN). An enemy motor torpedo boat was seen patrolling near this buoy.

At 1740 A/8, sighted what is thought to be a formed French chasseur [submarine chaser] carrying out an A/S search. It is thought possible the vessel had been attracted by the noise of HMS Upright's singing propellers. (3)

9 Oct 1940 At 0600 B/9, in position 49°55'N, 00°20'E, HMS Upright (Lt. F.J. Brooks, RN) picked up HE similar to that give out by an enemy motor torpedo boat. The HE finally disappeared to the northward. Considered this was an enemy patrol at dawn. (3)

11 Oct 1940 At 0630 A/11, in position 49°58'N, 00°18'E, HMS Upright (Lt. F.J. Brooks, RN) heard HE thought to be of an enemy motor torpedo boat like two days ago. Nothing was seen though. Visibility was 5 miles.

At 1030 A/11, it was noticed that a constant air patrol was kept in the area HMS Upright was in. Again HE thought to be of an enemy motor torpedo boat was heard but again nothing was sighted through the periscope.

At 1605 A/11, the F.O.S. (Flag Officer Submarines) signal timed 1431 A/11 was received which reported enemy motor vessels of Dieppe and Boulogne. Lt. Brooks decided to remain in position, 5 miles from Fecamp.
At 1758 A/11, the Capt. S.5's signal timed 1711 A/11 was received with ordered HMS Upright to withdraw 10 miles to the north of her patrol area until 0500 A/12.

This was due to the fact that the destroyers HMS Jackal (Cdr. C.L. Firth, MVO, RN), HMS Jaguar (Lt.Cdr. J.F.W. Hine, RN), HMS Jupiter (Cdr. D.B. Wyburd, RN), HMS Kelvin (Cdr. J.H. Allison, DSO, RN) and HMS Kipling (Cdr. A. St. Clair-Ford, RN) were to proceed from Portsmouth around 1830 A/11 to carry out a high speed sweep along the French coast between Le Havre and Dieppe following which they were to return to Plymouth at 0800 A/12. (3)

12 Oct 1940 At 1130 A/12, north-north-east of Fecamp, in position 49°49'N, 00°19'E and in poor visibility, HMS Upright (Lt. F.J. Brooks, RN) sighted eight motor torpedo boats in two columns disposed abeam. Trim was lost when the enemy boats were at a range of 1000 yards and HMS Upright bottomed in 80 feet to avoid speeding up. The enemy stopped in the vicinity for about 15 minutes before getting underway again.

This were not motor torpedo boats though but motor minesweepers from the 3rd R-boat Flotilla, made up of R 33, R 34, R 35, R 36, R 37, R 38, R 39, R 40 which were on a sweep from Le Havre to the east and then back to Le Havre.
At 1200 A/12, HMS Upright returned to periscope depth. There was nothing in sight.
At midnight during the night of 12/13 October 1940, HMS Upright left patrol in accordance with Capt. S.5's signal timed 1004 A/11. (5)
13 Oct 1940 At 0930 A/13, HMS Upright (Lt. F.J. Brooks, RN) ended her 1st war patrol at Portsmouth (Gosport / Fort Blockhouse / HMS Dolphin). (3)
25 Oct 1940 HMS Upright (Lt. F.J. Brooks, RN) is docked in AFD 2 at Haslar Creek, Gosport (Fort Blockhouse, HMS Dolphin). (6)
28 Oct 1940 HMS Upright (Lt. F.J. Brooks, RN) is undocked. (6)

30 Oct 1940 Around 1430 A/30, HMS Upright (Lt. F.J. Brooks, RN) departed Portsmouth for Gibraltar. She was to join the Mediterranean Fleet. En-route she was to make a short 48-hour patrol in the Bay of Biscay making this passage her 2nd war patrol.
For the daily positions of HMS Upright during this patrol see the map below.
9 Nov 1940 Around 2015 A/9, HMS Upright (Lt. F.J. Brooks, RN) arrived at Gibraltar. She was escorted in by HMS Arctic Ranger (Cdr.(Retd.) J.H. Young, RN) which had joined earlier that day. (7)

19 Nov 1940 Around 1915 A/19, HMS Upright (Lt. F.J. Brooks, RN) departed Gibraltar for her 3rd war patrol (1st in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol near Palermo, Sicily, Italy. Upon completion of the patrol she was to proceed to Malta.

26 Nov 1940 During the night of 26/27 November 1940 [most likely in the evening of the 26th] HMS Upright (Lt. F.J. Brooks, RN) sighted lights to the eastward of Ustica. HMS Upright altered course towards to investigate and the lights were identified as a Vichy-French troop convoy which had been mentioned in the C-in-C Mediterranean's signal timed 2351 B/23. One of the vessels was identified as the Champollion (12263 GRT, built 1925) and was closed to 800 yards so that her name could be read. The other ship in this convoy was the Mariette Pacha (12239 GRT, built 1926). (9)

1 Dec 1940 In the morning of 1 December 1940 [no time given in the patrol report and no details available in the log] HMS Upright (Lt. F.J. Brooks, RN) picked up strong HE passing close down the port side. The rate of change of bearing was very rapid and the type of HE was similar as to what was to be expected from a motor torpedo boat. The submarine at that time was at 60 feet. The HE quickly disappeared in the direction of Cape St. Vito. (8)
2 Dec 1940 P.M. on this day HMS Upright (Lt. F.J. Brooks, RN) left patrol to arrive at Malta at dawn on the 4th. (8)
4 Dec 1940 Around 0640 A/4, HMS Upright (Lt. F.J. Brooks, RN) ended her 3rd war patrol (1st in the Mediterranean) at Malta. (8)
7 Dec 1940 HMS Upright (Lt. F.J. Brooks, RN) is docked in No.5 graving dock at the Malta Dockyard. (10)
8 Dec 1940 HMS Upright (Lt. F.J. Brooks, RN) is undocked. (10)

18 Dec 1940 Around 1700 A/18, HMS Upright (with Lt.Cdr. J.H. Eaden, DSC, RN, in temporary command as Lt. Brooks was sick, in fact he did not return to Upright) departed Malta for her 4th war patrol (2nd in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol off Tripoli, Libya. She was to proceed through position 35°32'N, 14°12'E after leaving Malta.

19 Dec 1940 Following the disappearance of the Free French submarine Narval, HMS Upright (Lt.Cdr. J.H. Eaden, DSC, RN) is ordered to another patrol area near the Kerkenah bank, Tunisia, in case code books and other papers on board Narval have been compromised. (8)
22 Dec 1940 At 1800 A/22, HMS Upright (Lt.Cdr. J.H. Eaden, DSC, RN) left patrol to return to Malta. (8)
24 Dec 1940 Around 0700 A/24, HMS Upright (Lt.Cdr. J.H. Eaden, DSC, RN) ended her 4th war patrol (2nd in the Mediterranean) at Malta. (8)
31 Dec 1940 HMS Upright (Lt.Cdr. J.H. Eaden, DSC, RN) is docked in No.2 graving dock at the Malta Dockyard. (10)
3 Jan 1941 HMS Upright (Lt.Cdr. J.H. Eaden, DSC, RN) is undocked. (11)
7 Jan 1941 HMS Upright (Lt. E.D. Norman, DSC, RN) conducted exercises off Malta during which practice attacks were made on HMS Beryl (A/Cdr.(Retd.) J.C. Bird, DSC, RN) and HMS Jade (?). (11)
8 Jan 1941 Around 1715 A/8, HMS Upright (Lt. E.D. Norman, DSC, RN) departed Malta for her 5th war patrol (3rd in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol off Tripoli, Libya.
10 Jan 1941 At 0700 A/10, HMS Upright (Lt. E.D. Norman, DSC, RN) entered her patrol area to the north of Tripoli.
Between 1130 A/10 and 1230 A/10 and also between 1330 A/10 and 1710 A/10, many very distant bomb explosions were heard. (3)

12 Jan 1941 At 1055 A/12, when in approximate position 33°05'N, 13°12'E, HMS Upright (Lt. E.D. Norman, DSC, RN) sighted smoke bearing 165°. This appreared to be coming from two small vessels thought to be minesweeping the inshore route and proceeding eastwards. They returned to tripoli around 1400 A/12. (3)

13 Jan 1941 In a signal timed 1855 A/13, the Cdr. S.1 informed HMS Upright (Lt. E.D. Norman, DSC, RN) and HMS Regent (Lt.Cdr. H.C. Browne, RN) of the following; Aircraft reported at 0840 A/13, four destroyers and eight merchant ships in position 37°40'N, 11°44'E, course 228°, speed 10 knots.

It seems that the eight merchant ships must have been a mistake as this signal seem the refer to the convoy made up of the Italian troop transports Conte Rosso (17897 GRT, built 1922), Esperia (11398 GRT, built 1920), Marco Polo (12567 GRT, built 1912) and the Italian transport Calitea (4013 GRT, built 1933) which were on passage from Naples to Tripoli. At the time the convoy was sighted it was escorted by the Italian destroyers Ugolino Vivaldi, Luca Tarigo, Lanzerotto Malocello and Antonio da Noli.

On departure from Naples the convoy was escorted by the Italian torpedo boats Rosolino Pilo and Enrico Cosenz sailed from Naples at 1730 B/12-1900 B/12. At 0600 B/13-0715 B/13, the destroyers Ugolino Vivaldi, Luca Tarigo, Lanzerotto Malocello and Antonio da Noli sailed from Trapani to relieve the escort of the convoy off Marettimo. Enrico Cosenz turned back and returned to Naples at 2255 B/13, Rosolino Pilo arrived at Trapani at 1330 B/13. The convoy arrived at Tripoli at 1034 B/14-1230 B/14. (12)

14 Jan 1941 At 1100 A/14, when in approximate position 33°11'N, 13°10'E, Çi>HMS Upright (Lt. E.D. Norman, DSC, RN) sighted three small vessels leaving Tripoli on a course of 010° at a speed of 8 knots. Avoiding action was taken. The vessels turned out to be minesweepers (converted tugs). They were displaying two black balls at their yardarms. No depth charge chutes or throwers could be seen on board these vessels.
At 1200 A/12, these auxiliary minesweepers altered course to 190° on reaching position 33°10'N, 13°15'E. (3)
17 Jan 1941 At 1830 A/17, HMS Upright (Lt. E.D. Norman, DSC, RN) left patrol to return to Malta. She was to proceed through positions 33°55'N, 13°50'E and 35°58'N, 14°07'E during the return trip. (3)
19 Jan 1941 Around 0700 A/19, HMS Upright (Lt. E.D. Norman, DSC, RN) ended her 5th war patrol (3rd in the Mediterranean) at Malta. (3)

29 Jan 1941 Around 1500 A/29, HMS Upright (Lt. E.D. Norman, DSC, RN) departed Malta for her 6th war patrol (4th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol off the Kerkennah shallows off the east coast of Tunisia.
For the daily and attack positions of HMS Upright during this patrol see the map below.

1 Feb 1941 At 0920 A/1, in approximate position 34°48'N, 11°53'E, HMS Upright (Lt. E.D. Norman, DSC, RN) sighted a small coastal steamer at a range of 8 miles. She was inside the Kerkennah bank buoys. This may well have been a Vichy-French vessel. (3)

5 Feb 1941 HMS Upright (Lt. E.D. Norman, DSC, RN) makes a torpedo attack on an Italian convoy off the Kerkennah shallows off the Tunisian east coast. Four torpedoes were fired at a 4000 tons merchant vessel but these all missed the target.

The target was probably the German transport Wachtfels (8467 GRT, built 1928) escorted by the Italian armed merchant cruiser Caralis (3510 GRT, built 1928) on passage from Tripoli to Naples. They sailed from Tripoli at 1700 B/4 and arrived at Naples at 1430 B/6.

0215 A/5 - In position 34°53'N, 11°51'E sighted two darkened trawlers bearing 285° at a range of 5000 yards. They were proceeding on course 320° at 13 knots. While manoeuvring for an attack a flashes from a dimmed lantern were seen from a vessel beyond these trawlers. Fire was withheld as it was hoped a more juicy target was following these trawlers.
0240 A/5 - The trawlers were lost from sight.
0254 A/5 - Sighted two darkened ships bearing 140°. Range 7000 yards, enemy course 320°, estimated speed of the enemy 13 knots. Started attack.

0312 A/5 - Fired four torpedoes from 1200 yards. The target was a merchant vessel of about 4000 tons with one escort. The second torpedo had a gyro failure and ran about 30° to Port. If this had not been the case this torpedo would most likely have hit the target. The first torpedo missed ahead and the other two astern. The attack appear to be not noticed by the enemy. The range was opened up and the torpedo tubes were reloaded in the hope of another attack. However after 20 minutes the chase had to be abandoned. (3)
6 Feb 1941 In a signal timed 2031 A/6, the Cdr. S.1 ordered HMS Upright (Lt. E.D. Norman, DSC, RN) to leave patrol P.M. on the 7th and return to Malta. (13)
8 Feb 1941 At 1115 A/8, HMS Upright (Lt. E.D. Norman, DSC, RN) ended her 6th war patrol (4th in the Mediterranean) at Malta. (3)
17 Feb 1941 HMS Upright (Lt. E.D. Norman, DSC, RN) conducted hydroplane trials off Malta. (14)

19 Feb 1941 HMS Upright (Lt. E.D. Norman, DSC, RN) departed Malta for her 7th war patrol (5th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol off Kerkenah, Tunisia in area 'G' (34°10'N, 10°40'E - 000° - 50 - 70).
Before proceeding on patrol A/S exercises were carried out off Malta with the British destroyers HMS Havock (Cdr. R.E. Courage, DSO, DSC, RN) and HMS Hotspur (Lt.Cdr. C.P.F. Brown, DSC, RN). (3)
19 Feb 1941 For the daily and attack positions of HMS Upright during her 7th war patrol see the map below.
22 Feb 1941 At 0300 A/22, in position 34°21'N, 11°00'E, HMS Upright (Lt. E.D. Norman, DSC, RN) sighted the stern light of a vessel proceeding towards Sfax.
At 0535 A/22, in position 34°25'N, 10°47'E, several small fishing vessels were sighted ahead.

At 0715 A/22, in position 34°28'N, 10°47'E, a merchant vessel was seen steering 180°, bearing 090° at a range of 6 miles. This merchant vessel altered course to round No.8 buoy and steered to the south-east.
This was most probably a Vichy-French vessel.

At 1120 A/22, in position 34°36'N, 10°37'E, 4 miles from Sfax roads. There were no ships anchored in the roads and only a few masts discernible inside the harbour. Large numbers of small sailing fishing vessels made the trip up and down the bay. (3)
23 Feb 1941 HMS Upright (Lt. E.D. Norman, DSC, RN) torpedoed and sank the Italian transport Silvia Tripcovich (2365 GRT, built 1926) near the Kerkennah Bank in position 34°23'N, 11°49'E.

She had sailed from Trapani for Tripoli with the Italian transport Sabbia (5788 GRT, built 1926) escorted by the torpedo boat Generale Carlo Montanari. However, Sabbia had been torpedoed earlier by HMS Ursula (Lt. A.J. Mackenzie, RN) and Montanari had stayed behind to give her assistance. Silvia Tripcovich proceeded ahead independently when she had the misfortune of encountering HMS Upright. There were no survivors.
The convoy had sailed from Palermo at 0805 B/21. After her torpedoing, Sabbia managed to do 6 knots, and escorted by the torpedo boat, she reached Tripoli at 1316 B/24. ?

0240 A/23 - In position 34°23'N, 11°49'E sighted a darkened unescorted merchant vessel bearing 355°, course 175°. While attempting to cross the bow the target altered course to 200° and later zigged to 150°. Started attack.

0253 A/23 - Fired two torpedoes. The first missed ahead and the second hit just abaft the funnel causing a double explosion after which the flames leapt to 100 feet and then spread the whole length of the ship.
0315 A/23 - HMS Upright was dived by now. The target could be seen settling in the water and still burning. Surfaced and proceeded to the South-East at full speed to clear the area. (3)

25 Feb 1941 HMS Upright (Lt. E.D. Norman, DSC, RN) torpedoed and sank the Italian light cruiser Armando Diaz near the Kerkennah Bank in position 34°33'N, 11°45'E. Upright missed the Italian destroyer Ascari in the same attack.

The Armando Diaz was company with the light cruiser Giovanni delle Bande Nere and escorted by the destroyers Corazziere and Ascari and were covering the Marburg-convoy. Ascari was missed in the same attack and dropped six depth charges in one run followed by four more in another.

The Italian squadron had sailed from Palermo at 0400 B/24-0545 B/24. Ascari picked up 153 survivors and 9 bodies of Armando Diaz and was detached to Tripoli where she arrived at 1815 B/25. Giovanni delle Bande Nere, screened by Corazziere, returned to Palermo at 0917 B/26-1000 B/26.

0222 A/25 - In position 34°33'N, 11°45'E sighted three vessel bearing 315°, distance about 2.5 nautical miles. Started attack. While closing it was seen that the targets were two cruisers escorted by a destroyer, all in line ahead.
0240 A/25 - Fired four torpedoes at the second ship of the line. This was the largest ship. Dived after firing.
0241 A/25 - Heard a heavy explosion which was also observed through the periscope. Went to 130 feet.
0245 A/25 - The first depth charges were being dropped.
0330 A/25 - The 12th and last depth charge was dropped. Only the 10th and 11th depth charge were close but no damage was sustained.
0615 A/25 - Returned to periscope depth. Two modern Italian destroyers could be seen circling and stopping at the scene of the attack.
0815 A/25 - The destroyers made off the the west-north-west. With the area now severely compromised and with only two torpedoes left it was decided to return to Malta.
26 Feb 1941 Around 1750 A/26, HMS Upright (Lt. E.D. Norman, DSC, RN) ended her 7th war patrol (5th in the Mediterranean) at Malta.
She was escorted in by HMS Jade (?) which had joined around 1400 A/26. (3)

6 Mar 1941 At 0001 B/6, HMS Upright (Lt. E.D. Norman, DSC, RN) departed Malta for her 8th war patrol (6th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol off Tripoli, Libya in sector 'Z' between bearings 030° and 090° from the Tripoli lighthouse.

9 Mar 1941 In signals 1141 B/9 and 1212 B/9, HMS Upright (Lt. E.D. Norman, DSC, RN) was informed by the Cdr. S.1, that at 0730 B/9 enemy merchant ships had passed Kelibia Roads proceeding southwards at 14 knots. HMS Upright was also given freedom of action in sector 'Y'.
At 2148 B/9, in position 33°10'N, 13°20'E, a destroyer was sighted bearing 330° at a range of 3 miles. Enemy course was 060°. HMS Upright dived to avoid being sighted surfacing again at 2228 B/9.
This was probably either the torpedo boat Clio or Centauro. [See the event for 10 March 1941 for their movements.]? (3)

10 Mar 1941 At 0315 B/10, in position 33°14'N, 13°14'E, HMS Upright (Lt. E.D. Norman, DSC, RN) sighted two destroyers bearing 250° at a range of 5 miles. Enemy course was 330°. They were closed to try to identify them after HMS Upright dived at 0321 B/10 to avoid detection surfacing again at 0353 B/10.
These destroyers were not attacked as it was considered likely they were going out to meet a convoy.

These may have been either the Italian torpedo boats Clio and Centauro proceeding northwards from Tripoli to join the Andrea Gritti-convoy or Cigno and Castore proceeding northwards from Tripoli to reinforce the escort of the Vivaldi convoy.
From 0705 B/10 to 0735 B/10, while in position 33°16'N, 13°07'E, ten explosions were heard at three-minute intervals.
At 0900 B/10, in position 33°16'N, 13°10'E, two aircraft were sighted to the northward and more explosions heralded the arrival of a convoy.

At 0915 B/10, a convoy was sighted. This was later seen to be made up of four merchant vessels, escorted by four destroyers, two Navigatori-class and two Oriani-class. Three aircraft were patrolling over the convoy. HMS Upright closed at full speed to get into an attack position.

When next observed the convoy had altered course to 270° placing HMS Upright on their port quarter. During this time depth control was lost and when it was possible to have another look through the periscope the convoy had altered course to 160° and the attack was recommenced and Upright closed at high speed but at depth to avoid being sighted by the aircraft.

The next observation showed the convoy steering 090° at a range of 8000 yards. Shortly after this trim was again lost and HMS Upright dived to 150 feet. Trim was only regained 10 minutes later when the convoy was observed on course 150° placing HMS Upright nearly astern of the convoy.
At 1020 B/10, the attack was broken off and HMS Upright set course for the north-east.
This was the Vivaldi-convoy. [See below for more info.]
At 1300 B/10, in position 33°17'N, 13°14'E, two merchant vessels were sighted bearing 270° at a range of 10 miles. They were steering south.
This was the Fulmine-convoy. [See below for more info.]
From 1530 B/10 to 1600 B/10 several explosions were heard.

Italian movements in this area; At 1800 B/9-1900 B/9, the Italian torpedo boats Clio and Centauro sailed from Tripoli via Point A (318° - Tripoli Light – 15 miles) for a night A/S sweep between positions 33°15'N and 33°52'N and 12°46'N, 13°36'E, ahead of the arrival of several convoys. At 0700 B/10, in position 33°51'N, 13°09'E, they were first to meet the convoy made up of the transports Capo Vita (Italian, 5683 GRT, built 1916), Caffaro (6,476 GRT, built 1924), Fenicia (2,584 GRT, built 1919), and the Italian naval water tanker Tanaro (1,430 tons, built 1941), escorted by the Italian armed merchant cruiser Attilio Deffenu (3510 GRT, built 1910) and the torpedo boat Generale Achille Papa. They had sailed from Naples at 1910 B/6-1920 B/6 and arrived off Trapani at 2230 B/7. Because of the bad weather, Generale Achille Papa requested to proceed to the anchorage at Favignana which was better protected and Marina Trapani acquiesced at 0045 B/8, and all the vessels, except Attilio Deffenu, proceeded accordingly. They sailed from Favignana at 1115 B/8, arriving at Trapani at 1225 B/8. At 2000 B/8, Capo Vita and Fenicia escorted by Attilio Deffenu and Generale Achille Papa proceeded to Tripoli, however at 2055 B/8, Generale Achille Papa was recalled to Trapani. Only Attilio Deffenu would arrive at the destination; Capo Vita was sunk by HMS Utmost at 1201 B/9, and Fenicia was sunk by HMS Unique on at 0702 B/10. Tanaro, Caffaro and the German transport Alicante (2140 GRT, built 1934), escorted by the torpedo boats Generale Achille Papa and Circe sailed from Trapani at 1940 B/10. At 01455 B/12, the torpedo boat Cigno sailed from Tripoli and joined the convoy 1616 B/12 to relieve Generale Achille Papa which was towing a seaplane in distress and was given the permission to proceed independently. Generale Achille Papa arrived at Tripoli at 1930 B/12, the convoy followed at 1955 B/12-2235 B/12.

Clio and Centauro had been ordered then to reinforce the escort of the Vivaldi-convoy, which was made up of the German transports Marburg (7564 GRT, built 1928), Kybfels (7764 GRT, built 1937), Reichenfels (7744 GRT, built 1936) and Ankara (4768 GRT, built 1937) screened by the Italian destroyers Ugolino Vivaldi, Antonio da Noli, Lanzerotto Malocello and Lampo. This convoy had sailed from Naples at 1510 B/6 and arrived at Palermo at 0600 B/8 and sailed again at 0230 B/9 for Tripoli. The two torpedo boats were then to reinforce the escort of the Fulmine-convoy. This convoy was made up of the German transports Arcturus (2586 GRT, built 1937), Wachtfels (8467 GRT, 1928), Alicante (2140 GRT, built 1934) and the Italian transport Rialto (6099 GRT, built 1927) screened by the Italian destroyers Fulmine, Turbine and Baleno. They had sailed from Naples at 1310 B/8-1410 B/8. At 0500 B/10, Clio and Centauro sighted the torpedo boats Cigno and Castore proceeding northward (they had sailed from Tripoli at 0000 B/10-0005 B/10 to reinforce the escort of the Vivaldi-convoy which they sighted at 0640 B/10). At 0700 B/10, Clio and Centauro were in position 33°51'N, 13°09'E where they were waiting for the convoy. At 0726 B/10, they were informed of the torpedoing of Fenicia and proceeded to her assistance. At 0848 B/10, they spotted the Fulmine-convoy and at 0935 B/10 sighted the armed merchant cruiser Attilio Deffenu which informed them that Fenicia had sunk and the survivors had been picked up. Clio and Centauro sighted the destroyer Baleno which was cruising in the area and informed her that they were now proceeding to reinforce the Fulmine and Turbine convoy as per their original orders. At 1050 B/10, as they were joining this convoy, they were ordered by Marilibia to hunt the submarine that had sunk Fenicia. They required clarification and at 1249 B/10, Marilibia confirmed the order to leave the convoy and proceed to the A/S hunt. At 1552 B/10, they were on the spot where Fenicia had sunk and proceeded for an A/S search in an area between positions 34°02'N and 34°31'N and 12°27'E and 12°53'E. Nothing was sighted except a drifting Italian mine which was found at 1832 B/10 in 34°26'N, 12°43'E and was sunk by 20mm/65 gunfire.
At 1200 B/10-1214 B/10, the four merchant ships of the Vivaldi-convoy and the destroyers Lanzerotto Malocello, Antonio da Noli and Lampo entered Tripoli. Ugolino Vivaldi arrived at 2340 B/10.
At 1545 B/10-1645 B/10, the Fulmine-convoy entered Tripoli.
Attilio Deffenu arrived at Tripoli at 1710 B/10.

At 0650 B/11, Clio and Centauro sighted a convoy consisting of the Italian transports Sebastiano Venier (6310 GRT, built 1940) and Andrea Gritti (6338 GRT, built 1939) escorted by the torpedo boats Pallade and Polluce and joined it. This convoy had sailed from Palermo at 0448 B/10-0500 B/10 escorted by the torpedo boat Pallade. Polluce had sailed from Palermo at 1905 B/9 and arrived at Lampedusa at 0758 B/10 and sailed from there at 2259 B/10 to join the convoy. They arrived at Tripoli at 1200 B/11-1332 B/11. (3)
12 Mar 1941 HMS Upright (Lt. E.D. Norman, DSC, RN) made a torpedo attack on an enemy convoy. Two torpedoes were fired at the largest of the two merchant vessels. No hits were obtained.
The convoy attacked was made up of the Italian transports Giulia (5921 GRT, built 1926) and Sabaudia (1703 GRT, built 1911). They were escorted by the Italian destroyers Turbine and Baleno.
1500 B/12 - While in position 33°47'N, 12°55'E, two explosions were heard.

1700 B/12 - In position 33°50'N, 12°55'E smoke was sighted on bearing 090°. On the same bearing two patrolling aircraft were seen. Range was about 12 miles. The ships were steering north and appeared to be on the Tripoli - Lampedusa route.
This smoke was perhaps from the Marburg-convoy if they actually travelled at more than 14 knots. [See below for more information].
At 1740 B/12, the Cdr. S.1's signal timed 1613 B/12 was received which ordered HMS Utmost to patrol in position 33°40'N, 12°45'E.

1925 B/12 - In position 33°56'N, 12°55'E sighted smoke bearing 180°, distance 10 nautical miles. Closed submerged at speed for 10 minutes. Returned to periscope depth to see if the ships were still there but it was too dark to see them through the periscope.

2020 B/12 - In position 33°56'N, 12°52'E surfaced and sighted one merchant vessel bearing 240°, range 5 nautical miles. Enemy course was 330°. Closed at maximum speed. Shortly afterwards sighted the convoy comprising 2 destroyers and one small and one large merchant vessel in line ahead. Started attack.

2033 B/12 - Fired two torpedoes from 3000 yards at the rear and largest merchant vessel. After firing turned away and ran off at full speed. The first torpedo was observed to run on the surface. No hits were obtained.
At 2100 B/12, course was set to proceed to position 33°40'N, 12°45'E as had been ordered in the Cdr. S.1's signal timed 1613 B/12.

There were three convoys which sailed from Tripoli on that day; 1) The Italian transports Giulia (5921 GRT, built 1925) and Sabaudia (1590 GRT, built 1911), screened by the Italian destroyers Turbine and Baleno which sailed from Tripoli 1115/12-1200 B/12. They were to have sailed via Lampedusa and Pantelleria at 11 knots (they actually proceeded at 10.5 knots) but at 1933 B/12, a Supermarina order was received to proceed via Kerkennah buoy no. 4 and they arrived at Naples at 2145 B/14-2200 B/14.

2) The German transports Marburg (7564 GRT, built 1928), Kybfels (7764 GRT, built 1937), Reichenfels (7744 GRT, built 1936) and Ankara (4768 GRT, built 1937) screened by the Italian destroyers Ugolino Vivaldi, Antonio da Noli, Lanzerotto Malocello. which sailed from Tripoli at 1300 B/12-1400 B/12 at 14 knots and arrived at Naples at 0635 B/14-0700 B/14 except Ankara, Kybfels and Antonio da Noli which arrived at Palermo at 2000 B/13.

3) The German transports Arcturus (2586 GRT, built 1937) and Wachtfels (8467 GRT, 1928) escorted by the Italian destroyers Lampo and Fulmine via (1) 340° - Sidi Bilal - 20 miles (approx. 33°08'N, 12°49'E) and (2) 110° - Kerkennah Buoy no.4 - 20 miles They had sailed from Tripoli at 1230 B/12-1400 B/12 at 12 knots via Lampedusa and Pantelleria and arrived at Naples at 1630 B/14-1800 B/14. (3)

13 Mar 1941 At 1200 B/13, HMS Upright (Lt. E.D. Norman, DSC, RN) left patrol to return to Malta proceeding through position 33°40'N, 12°45'E en-route as had been ordered in the Cdr. S.1's signal timed 1615 B/12.

At 2000 B/13, in position 34°06'N, 12°48'E, HE was picked up on bearing 130°. HMS Upright surfaced in a heavy rain squall. Visibility was only 1000 yards. One merchant vessel was sighted bearing 040° steering northwards. No attack was made as the state of the weather and low visibility made the chances of success very small, and the target was not sufficiently valuable.

This was probably the Italian motor tanker Caucaso (2065 GRT, built 1920) which sailed from Tripoli escorted by the Italian armed merchant cruiser Attilio Deffenu (3510 GRT, built 1920) at 0700 B/13 and reached Bizerte at 0730 B/15.

Another possibility, but very doubtful, is that it was ship for the convoy made up of the Italian tanker Rondine (6468 GRT, built 1924) and the Italian transport Cilicia (2747 GRT, built 1928) escorted by the Italian torpedo boat Clio. They had sailed from Tripoli at 0550 B/13 at 9 knots but at 1800 B/13 they were in position 34°05'N, 12°55'E (quite near HMS Upright) but had to turn back due to the high waves encountered. At 1805 B/13, they sighted the Italian torpedo boat Centauro which had been sent by Marilibia to reinforce the escort and sailed from Tripoli at 1200 B/13 (with Polluce which failed to reach them and returned to Tripoli at 2256 B/13). The convoy returned to Tripoli at 0830 B/14-0835 B/14. (15)
15 Mar 1941 At 0900 B/15, HMS Upright (Lt. E.D. Norman, DSC, RN) ended her 8th war patrol (6th in the Mediterranean) at Malta. (3)
19 Mar 1941 HMS Upright (Lt. E.D. Norman, DSC, RN) is docked in No.5 graving dock at the Malta Dockyard. (16)
22 Mar 1941 HMS Upright (Lt. E.D. Norman, DSC, RN) is undocked. (16)
28 Mar 1941 HMS Upright (Lt. E.D. Norman, DSC, RN) conducted trials and / or exercises off Malta. (16)

29 Mar 1941 At 1845 B/29, HMS Upright (Lt. E.D. Norman, DSC, RN) departed Malta for her 9th war patrol (7th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol between Kerkennah, Tunisia and Tripoli, Libya. (Area 'A', 10 miles either side of a line joining positions 33°30'N, 12°58'E and 34°44'N, 11°56'E.)
On departure a practice attack was carried out on HMS Jade (?). (3)
29 Mar 1941 For the daily and attack positions of HMS Upright during her 9th war patrol see the map below.

31 Mar 1941 HMS Upright (Lt. E.D. Norman, DSC, RN) torpedoed and damaged the German transport Galilea (1927 GRT, built 1922) about 60 nautical miles north-west of Tripoli, Libya in position 33°38'N, 12°40'E.

The convoy attacked was, besides the Galilea made up of the following transports Aquitania (Italian, 4971 GRT, built 1924), Caffaro (Italian, 6476 GRT, built 1924), Beatrice C. (Italian, 7015 GRT, built 1920). They were escorted by the Italian torpedo boats Clio, Calliope and Pegaso. The convoy was on passage from Tripoli to Naples.

The convoy sailed from Tripoli via the Ras Bilal route at 2020 B/30-0045 B/31. After the torpedoing, Galilea was beached in Tripoli at 0900 B/1. The torpedo boats Pleiadi and Cigno sailed from Tripoli at 1225 B/1-1330 B/1 and reinforced the convoy at 1800 B/1 in position 34°31'N, 12°47'E. They were replacing Pegaso and Calliope which had sailed back with the damaged Galilea). They arrived at Naples at 2005-2400 B/2 April.

Two of the crew of the Galilea were killed and three wounded, she was initially taken in tow by torpedo boat Pegaso and escorted by torpedo boat Calliope. Later, the tug Polifemo arrived and took over the tow and she was beached near Tripoli to prevent her from sinking. The damaged Galitea was never repaired.
0300 B/31 - Entered area 'A'.

0625 B/31 - In position 33°39'N, 12°40'E, while Upright was submerged, heard HE bearing 075°. Came to periscope depth and saw two objects on that bearing but they disappeared after a few minutes before they could be identified.
0700 B/31 - In position 33°38'N, 12°40'E sighted three merchant vessels escorted by two Navigatori-class destroyers bearing 220° on a course of 350°. Started attack.

0728 B/31 - By now it had became clear that all three merchants were in ballast. A fourth merchant was now spotted three miles astern of the others. This merchant appeared to be partially laden so this one was selected as the target. Another destroyer was seen to escort this vessel.
0739 B/31 - Fired two torpedoes from 1000 yards at the merchant vessel that was thought to be of about 5000 tons. One hit was obtained.
0751 B/31 - A counterattack followed with four depth charges.
0806 B/31 - Two more depth charges were dropped that were quite close and caused minor damage.
0855 B/31 - Returned to periscope depth and saw the target to be low in the water and down by the stern. Two destroyers were seen in company.
0900 B/31 - An aircraft was seen patrolling the area. Went to 150 feet.
0921 B/31 - Another depth charge attack by the destroyers. It appears Upright was spotted from the air at 0900 hours. The depth charges however caused no damage.
1130 B/31 - All HE faded out.
1230 B/31 - Returned to periscope depth. Nothing in sight. (3)

4 Apr 1941 At 1300 B/4, in position 34°00'N, 12°32'E, HMS Upright (Lt. E.D. Norman, DSC, RN) sighted an aircraft and shortly after two merchant vessels were also sighted. They were apparently not escorted by surface vessels. No HE could be heard. An attack was not possible. Lt. Norman decided to remain in the vicinity hoping more unescorted ships would come his way.

We have been unable to identify these vessels. There were movements towards Tripoli on this afternoon. Two German transports, Samos (2576 GRT, built 1923) and Adana (4176 GRT, built 1922), screened by the Italian destroyers Folgore and Strale, had returned to Tripoli earlier in the day after being recalled when Force H was discovered to be at sea. (3)

7 Apr 1941 The Cdr. S.1., in a signal timed 1902 B/7, informed HMS Upholder (Lt.Cdr. M.D. Wanklyn, RN) and HMS Upright (Lt. E.D. Norman, DSC, RN) that two enemy transports and two escorts had been reported at 1448 B/7 in position 33°45'N, 13°13'E proceeding on course 315° at 10 knots.

This must have referred to the convoy on passage from Tripoli to Naples and made up of the transports Amsterdam (Italian, 10873 GRT, built 1921) and Giulia (Italian, 5921 GRT, built 1926). On departure from Tripoli they were escorted by the Italian torpedo boats Calliope, Castore. They had sailed from Tripoli at 0522-0541 B/7 for Naples, the two torpedo boats were to escort the convoy as far as Marettimo, but the bad weather forced them to leave the convoy at 0905 B/8 to take refuge at Sousse where they dropped anchor in the roads at 1525 B/8. They were visited by a French Capitaine de corvette and explained their predicament. They sailed again at 0500 B/9 and reached Trapani at 1441-1540 B/9. The two merchant ships appear to have continued unescorted until near Naples. The torpedo boat Generale Antonio Cascino sailed from Naples at 1320 B/9 to act as a target for exercises with torpedo bombers and subsequently escorted in the two merchant ships, arriving in Naples at 2020 B/9. (17)

8 Apr 1941 HMS Upright (Lt. E.D. Norman, DSC, RN) made a torpedo attack on an enemy convoy about 100 nautical miles north-north-west of Tripoli, Libya in position 34°30'N, 12°51'E. No hits were obtained.

This must have been the convoy made up of the Italian troop transports (liners) Esperia (11398 GRT, built 1920), Conte Rosso (17879 GRT, built 1922), Marco Polo (12567 GRT, built 1912) and Victoria (13098 GRT, built 1931), escorted by the destroyers Luca Tarigo, Euro, Baleno and Lampo. The convoy sailed from Tripoli at 1515 B/7-1700 B/7 and arrived at Naples at 0630 B/9-0725 B/9. At 0030 B/8, Luca Tarigo heard an underwater explosion. Conte Rosso also heard explosions.
(All times are zone -2) 0005 B/8 - In position 34°30'N, 12°51'E sighted a convoy of three merchant vessels and two escorts bearing 143° on a course of 350°. Started attack.

0021 B/8 - Fired two torpedoes at the two leading merchant vessels which overlapped each other and presented a single target. Then fired two torpedoes at the rear and largest merchant vessel. No hits were obtained and no reaction of the escort followed so the attack seems to have been unobserved.
0145 B/8 - Dived to reload tubes 3 and 4.
0215 B/8 - Surfaced and set course for Malta. (3)
9 Apr 1941 Around 0700 B/9, HMS Upright (Lt. E.D. Norman, DSC, RN) ended her 9th war patrol (7th in the Mediterranean) at Malta. (3)
26 Apr 1941 HMS Upright (Lt. R.S. Brookes, DSC, RN) conducted attack exercises off Malta during which HMS Beryl (A/Cdr.(Retd.) J.C. Bird, DSC, RN) acted as target.
Spare Commanding Officer Lt. Brookes had temporary taken command of HMS Upright as Lt. Norman was unfit and indeed for a rest. (18)

27 Apr 1941 Around 1245 B/27, HMS Upright (Lt. R.S. Brookes, DSC, RN) departed Malta for her 10th war patrol (8th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol between Kerkennah, Tunisia and Tripoli, Libya on a patrol line 4 - 34°40'N, 11°40'E - 090° - 10 - 180° - 40 with HMS Upholder (Lt.Cdr. M.D. Wanklyn, RN) in position 'W' (34°40'N, 11°40'E), HMS Ursula (Lt. A.J. Mackenzie, RN) in position 'X' (34°40'N, 11°52'E), HMS Upright in position 'Y' (34°40'N, 12°04'E) and HMS Truant (Lt.Cdr. H.A.V. Haggard, RN) in position 'Z' (34°40'N, 12°18'E). This patrol line was deployed to intercept an important southbound enemy convoy.

This convoy was made up of the transports Marburg (German, 7564 GRT, built 1928), Kybfels (German, 7764 GRT, built 1937), Reichenfels (German, 7744 GRT, built 1936), Birmania (Italian, 5305 GRT, built 1930) and Rialto (Italian, 6099 GRT, built 1927) from Naples to Tripoli. It was escorted by the Italian destroyers Fulmine, Euro and the Italian torpedo boats Canopo, Castore and Procione.

The convoy sailed from Naples at 2400 B/24-0225 B/25 for Tripoli but the bad weather forced them to take refuge at Palermo at 1700 B/25-2130 B/25. They were to be joined by the Italian troop transport (liner) Marco Polo (12272 GRT, built 1912) escorted by the torpedo boat Orsa which had sailed from Messina at 2016 B/25-2030 B/25 and arrived at Palermo at 0710 B/26-0737 B/26. The light cruisers Giovanni delle Bande Nere and Luigi Cadorna (4th Cruiser Division) screened by the destroyers Maestrale and Scirocco arrived at Palermo at 1620 B/25. They may have been initially selected as a covering force. The interruption of the passage was due to the sighting by seven Axis aircraft of two battleships and an aircraft carrier at 0950 B/27 on an indeterminate course, 15 miles NW of La Galite. The passage of the convoy which was to have been carried out by rounding the Sicilian west coast (Marettimo route) was changed to go through the Straits of Messina. They sailed from Palermo at 0346 B/28-0530 B/28. Birmania and Rialto escorted by the destroyers Euro and Fulmine arrived at Messina at 1850 B/28-1950 B/28 and sailed from there at 0400 B/30. Marco Polo, Marburg, Kybfels and Reichenfels, escorted by Canopo, Orsa, Castore and Procione which arrived at Augusta at 1950 B/28-2015 B/28 and sailed from Augusta at 0045 B/30-0200 B/30). They arrived at Tripoli at 2120 B/1-2240 B/1. They were covered by the heavy cruisers Trieste, Bolzano (Third Cruiser Division) screened by the destroyers Ascari, Carabiniere and Vincenzo Gioberti which sailed from Messina at 1255-1330 B/30, and proceeded westward, north of the Sicilian coast. They returned to Messina at 2330 B/2-0047 B/3 May. The light cruisers Luigi di Savoia Duca degli Abruzzi and Giuseppe Garibaldi (Eighth Cruiser Squadron) screened by the destroyers Alpino, Fuciliere and Bersagliere sailed from Palermo at 1722 B/30-1810 B/30. At 1920 B/30, they sighted the Third Cruiser Division and the two squadrons joined up at 2026 B/30. At 0830 B/1, they were off Linosa and, at 1015 B/1, off Lampedusa. At 1300 B/1, they sighted the Marco Polo convoy which was escorted until 1811 B/1 when the two cruiser divisions left the convoy and turned northward. The Eighth Cruiser Division returned to Palermo at 1905 B/2-1945 B/2. The Third Cruiser Division returned to Messina at 2330 B/2-0047 B/3. The light cruisers Eugenio di Savoia, Emanuele Filiberto Duca d'Aosta (Seventh Cruiser Division), screened by the destroyers Nicolò Zeno, Alvise da Mosto, Giovanni da Verazzano and Antonio Pigafetta sailed from Taranto at 0415-0530 B/30 for a minelaying operation (part of the T minefield off Tripoli) and returned to Augusta at 0635 B/2-0710 B/2.
Lt. Brookes was temporary in command of HMS Upright as Lt. Norman was not fit and in need for a rest.
28 Apr 1941 At 1200 B/28, HMS Upright (Lt. R.S. Brookes, DSC, RN) arrived in position 'Y' (34°40'N, 12°04'E) and commenced patrol to the east and south of this position. (3)

28 Apr 1941 In a signal timed 2305 B/28, the Cdr. S.1 sent the following orders to the four submarines on the patrol line, HMS Upholder (Lt.Cdr. M.D. Wanklyn, RN), HMS Ursula (Lt. A.J. Mackenzie, RN), HMS Upright (Lt. R.S. Brookes, DSC, RN) and HMS Truant (Lt.Cdr. H.A.V. Haggard, RN); While submerged submarines should patrol north and south in the vicinity of their respective positions and not east and west. Freedom of action allowed at night as weather conditions dictate. Today's air rec reconnaissance report was blank. (17)

1 May 1941 At 0521 B/1, sights showed that HMS Upright (Lt. R.S. Brookes, DSC, RN) was three miles to the north of the patrol line. Course was set to 200° to return to position 'Y' (34°40'N, 12°04'E).
At 0549 B/1, HE was picked up on bearing 310° but it was only picked up for 30 seconds before it faded. It was still too dark to see anything.
At 0605 B/1, in position 41°41'N, 12°04'E, HE was picked up on bearing 317° but again it was only picked up for 30 seconds. A good all round look showed nothing in sight.
From around 0800 B/1, the sea state made it impossible to keep periscope depth.

At 1055 B/1, in position 34°37'N, 12°06'E, two loud explosions were heard. Nothing was in sight. Explosions continued at intervals for some time. While patrolling on course 180° and 360° through position 'Y' it appeared that these explosions were passing away to the south.

At 1500 B/1, the Vice Admiral Malta's signal timed 1340 B/1, was received which reported four enemy cruisers and six enemy destroyers in position 35°10'N, 12°38'E on course 210° at 15 knots at 1130 B/1. These ships passed well to the eastward of HMS Upright.

This were; 1) The heavy cruisers Trieste, Bolzano (Third Cruiser Division) screened by the destroyers Ascari, Carabiniere and Vincenzo Gioberti. 2) The light cruisers Luigi di Savoia Duca degli Abruzzi and Giuseppe Garibaldi (Eighth Cruiser Division) screened by the destroyers Alpino, Fuciliere and Bersagliere. [For their movements see the event for 27 April 1941.]
At 2123 B/1, the Cdr. S.1's signal timed 1251 B/1 was received which ordered HMS Upright to patrol in area 'U' (33°20'N, 11°00'E - 000° - 40 - 60). Course was set to comply.

At 2255 B/1, the Cdr. S.1's signal timed 2003 B/1 was received which ordered HMS Upright not to leave her current patrol position until 0600 B/2. Lt. Brookes decided to patrol on a line 180° - 360° through her current position. (19)

2 May 1941 HMS Upright (Lt. R.S. Brookes, DSC, RN) tried to attack a convoy about 50 nautical miles south-east of the Kerkennah shallows. Due to an mistake in the setup of the attack it had to be broken off. Later one torpedo was fired from great range but it missed.

The convoy in question was probably the convoy consisting of the transports Tilly M. Russ (German, 1600 GRT, built 1926), Brook (German, 1225 GRT, built 1927), Bainsizza (Italian, 7933 GRT, built 1930), the tanker Sanandrea (Italian, 5077 GRT, built 1908) and the German tug Max Behrendt escorted by the Italian torpedo boats Generale Carlo Montanari, Clio and the armed merchant cruiser Ramb III.

This convoy sailed from Palermo at 2200 B/30-0000 B/1. The torpedo boat Generale Carlo Montanari sailed from Trapani at 0646 B/1 May and at 0843 B/1 joined off Marettimo the convoy and, at the same time, the torpedo boat Enrico Cosenz was detached to Trapani where she arrived at 1100 B/1. At 1716 B/1, off Pantelleria, the convoy was reinforced by the Italian armed merchant cruiser Ramb III (3667 GRT, built 1938). At 1300 B/2, the convoy was attacked by four Blenheim bombers without suffering any damage. The torpedo boats Centauro and Polluce sailed from Tripoli at 1314 B/2-1325 B/2 and at 1900 B/2 reinforced the escort. They arrived at Tripoli at 0750 B/3-0920 B/3.
It appears that the convoy was escorted only by Ramb III, Generale Carlo Montanari and Clio at the time of the attack.

At 1403 B/2, the Vice Admiral Malta's signal timed 1312 B/2 was received. It stated that at 1125 B/2, five merchant ships and one destroyer had been sighted in position 34°40'N, 11°55'E proceeding on course 189°. It was expected that if the enemy kept this main course they could be intercepted around 1700 hours.
1623 B/2 - In position 33°59'N, 12°01'E sighted smoke bearing 260°. Altered course to investigate.
1638 B/2 - Sighted a convoy of five merchant ships, three of about 4000 tons and two smaller ones of about 2000 tons. They were escorted by two destroyers and two aircraft. Started attack.

1652 B/2 - Due to a faulty attack setup by Uprights inexperienced Commanding Officer she ended up 300 yards in front of one of the escorts when she was to fire torpedoes the attack had to be broken off and Upright was taken deep.
1728 B/2 - Returned to periscope depth. Saw one of the merchant vessels a bit behind the remainder of the convoy. Decided to attack despite the rather great range.
1743 B/2 - Fired one torpedo from 6000 yards at the last ship of the port column. It missed.
1843 B/2 - An enemy report, timed 1825 B/2 was passed. (3)

6 May 1941 At 1730 B/6, HMS Upright (Lt. R.S. Brookes, DSC, RN) received the Cdr. S.1's signal timed 1305 B/6. This stated the following; leave area 'U' at dusk on 6 may and proceed to operate on the coastal route in area 'V', south of 33°20'N and between 13°40'E and 15°00'E. HMS Truant (Lt.Cdr. H.A.V. Haggard, RN) reports minesweeper activity in the eastern approaches to Tripoli and about two small ships daily coast crawling eastward.
Course was set for the north-east corner of this new patrol area. (19)
7 May 1941 At 0815 B/7, HMS Upright (Lt. R.S. Brookes, DSC, RN), when she was in position 33°46'N, 12°48'E, heard a very loud explosion similar to ship being torpedoed.

It is to be expected that HMS Undaunted (Lt. J.L. Livesey, RN) was patrolling to the south of that position and may have something to do with the explosion heard. HMS Undaunted did not return from patrol. (20)
8 May 1941 At 2111 B/8, HMS Upright (Lt. R.S. Brookes, DSC, RN) received the Cdr. S.1's signal timed 1215 B/8, which ordered to leave patrol the following day and return to Malta. (19)
8 May 1941 At 1000 B/8, HMS Upright (Lt. R.S. Brookes, DSC, RN), when she was in position 32°33'N, 14°30'E, heard a series of distant explosions like a submarine was being depth charged.

It is to be expected that HMS Undaunted (Lt. J.L. Livesey, RN) was patrolling to the west of that position and may have something to do with the explosions heard. HMS Undaunted did not return from patrol. (20)
9 May 1941 At 1105 B/9, HMS Upright (Lt. R.S. Brookes, DSC, RN) left patrol and set course for Malta. (19)
11 May 1941 At 0715 B/11, HMS Upright (Lt. R.S. Brookes, DSC, RN) ended her 10th war patrol (8th in the Mediterranean) at Malta. (3)

24 May 1941 Around 2100 B/24, HMS Upright (Lt. E.D. Norman, DSC, RN) departed Malta for her 11th war patrol (9th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol off Calabria, Italy in area 'M'. She also carried a raiding party to blow up a railway ashore if the opportunity occurred. En-route to her patrol area she is to proceed through position 36°00'N, 14°52'E, 35°51'N, 15°15'E and 37°00'N, 16°50'E.

24 May 1941 In a signal timed 2358 B/24, the Vice Admiral Malta sent the following signal to HMS Upright (Lt. E.D. Norman, DSC, RN); Troop transports escorted by destroyers in position 36°45'N, 15°42'E at 2042 B/24 on course 180°, speed 18 knots. Attempt to intercept.
There is no mention of this signal in the patrol report of HMS Upright, so either she did not receive it or it was not possible to intercept the convoy reported.

The Italian troop transports (liners) Conte Rosso (17879 GRT, built 1922), Marco Polo (12272 GRT, built 1912), Victoria (13098 GRT, built 1931) and Esperia (11398 GRT, built 1920), escorted by the Italian destroyer Freccia and the torpedo boats Procione, Orsa and Pegaso sailed from Naples at 0250 B/24-0440 B/24. At 1610 B/24, the convoy crossed the Straits of Messina, reinforced by the Italian torpedo boats Calatafimi and Perseo which sailed from Messina at 1500 B/24-1517 B/24 and Calliope which had sailed from Trapani at 0500 B/24. The three torpedo boats only escorted them for a few hours through the Straits of Messina passage and returned to Messina at 2045 B/24-2100 B/24. The heavy cruisers Trieste, Bolzano (Third Cruiser Division) and the destroyers Corazziere, Lanciere and Ascari sailed from Messina at 1535-1650 B/24 to provide a close screen. At 2040 B/24, 270° - Murro di Porco - 10 miles, Conte Rosso was sunk by HMS Upholder (Lt.Cdr. M.D. Wanklyn, RN). Freccia had given the alarm and all ships turned to starboard, except Conte Rosso which turned to port and was torpedoed. Procione picked up 542 survivors (7 died on board) and brought them to Augusta at 0610 B/25, Pegaso followed at 0800 B/25. The destroyers Corazziere and Lanciere and the Italian hospital ship Arno (which was arriving from Benghazi) and later the Italian hospital Sicilia also proceeded to search for survivors, the two destroyers arriving at Augusta at 0315 B/25 and then returned to Messina at 1934 B/25-2035 B/25. There were 1297 victims from a total of 2729. At 0125 B/25, the torpedo boats Cigno, Pallade and Clio sailed from Messina to join the search, they returned at 1720 B/25. The Third Cruiser Division, the liners Marco Polo, Esperia and Victoria screened by Freccia, Ascari and Orsa proceeded on. On the morning of the 25th, Trieste, Bolzano and Ascari turned back and arrived at Messina at 1935 B/25-2020 B/25. At 2003 B/24, the torpedo boat Generale Carlo Montanari and at 0520 B/25 Climene sailed from Tripoli and at 1230 B/25 reinforced the convoy. At 0900 B/25, the torpedo boat Castore sailed from Tripoli and joined the convoy at 1410 B/25, taking the lead to pilot it to Tripoli. At 1351 B/25, in position 33°13'N, 13°59'E, Climene sighted an oil slick and attacked it with depth charges, claiming to have sighted a submarine briefly breaking surface and claiming it sunk (bogus, no submarine in this area) before rejoining the convoy at 1424 B/25. The three liners screened by Freccia, Orsa, Castore, Generale Carlo Montanari and Climene arrived at Tripoli at 1642 B/25-1754 B/25. (20)

25 May 1941 In a signal timed 0750 B/25, the Vice Admiral Malta ordered HMS Upright (Lt. E.D. Norman, DSC, RN) not to enter area 'M' until further orders. Following the sinking of the Italian troop transport Conte Rosso the previous day, it was to be expected that the Italians were conducting intese A/S searches in the area. (20)

27 May 1941 During the night of 27 / 28 May 1941, HMS Upright (Lt. E.D. Norman, DSC, RN) landed a raiding party (Lt. Schofield, Royal Fusiliers and five other ranks) near Torre Ellera, Calabria, Italy to sabotage a railway. The party successfully planted bombs. At 0412 B/28, HMS Upright retired to the south-east after the party had returned on board. At 0513 B/28 a rumbling noise was heard on the shore that was thought to be a train blowing up.

At 2340 B/27, when approaching the coast for the special operation and in position 33°19'N, 16°37'E, lights of a large vessel had been sighted bearing 090°. This ship was steering southwards and is thought to have been a hospital ship. Since the special operation was in progress it was not investigated.
This was probably the Italian hospital ship Toscana (9429 GRT, built 1923), which sailed from Venice in 1935 B/25 and arrived at Augusta at 1150 B/28.
Italian sources have not confirmed the result of the special operation. (21)

27 May 1941 In a signal timed 2232 B/27, the Cdr. S.1 sent the following orders to HMS Upright (Lt. E.D. Norman, DSC, RN); On completion of special operation retire to the south-east making brief report. Area 'M' severely compromised by Upholder's successful attacks off Cape del'Armi and position 36°45'N, 15°42'E. Approach area 'M' on latitude 37°40'N until reaching longitude 15° 40'E thence steer ??? [garbled in signal] to operate in area 'P' (35°50'N, 15°20'E - 000° - 40 - 50). Tripoli convoy passing through this area. (20)

28 May 1941 At 0525 B/28, in position 38°18'N, 16°34'E, HMS Upright (Lt. E.D. Norman, DSC, RN) sighted a single funnelled destroyer or torpedo boat and three trawlers bearing 300° at a range of 5 miles. Enemy course was 040°. The arrival of these ships so soon after the landing operation was completed can only have been coincidence. Avoiding action was taken.
Between 1000 B/28 and 1200 B/28, several distant underwater explosions were heard.
At 2310 B/28, after having retired well away from the coast and in position 37°50'N, 16°55'E, a signal timed 2301 B/28 was passed reporting the completion of the special operation. (21)

28 May 1941 In a signal timed 2102 B/28, the Cdr. S.1 informed HMS Unique (Lt. A.F. Collett, RN) and HMS Upright (Lt. E.D. Norman, DSC, RN) of the following; At 1540 B/28 reconnaissance aircraft detected three cruisers in position 36°20'N, 12°00'E on course 180° at 20 knots.
This were most likely the light cruiser Luigi Cadorna screened by the destroyers Maestrale and Grecale which sailed from Palermo at 1915 B/27-2010 B/27 and returned at 0605 B/29-06403 B/29.
At 1820 B/28, three cruisers, three destroyers and two small merchant ships were reported to be at Messina.

There were three heavy cruisers, three destroyers and three torpedo boats at Messina at 1820 B/28; Heavy cruisers: Bolzano (since 2000 B/25 May), Trento (since 1415 B/28 May) and Gorizia (since 1415 B/28 May) Destroyers: Ascari, Lanciere and Corazziere (all since 1415 B/28-1514 B/28 May) Torpedo boats: Giuseppe Dezza (since 0827 B/25 May) , Cassiopea (since 1920 B/27) and Procione (since 1655 B/28) (20)

29 May 1941 At 2200 B/29, in position 37°21'N, 16°13'E, HMS Upright (Lt. E.D. Norman, DSC, RN) sighted light bearing 260° at a range of 5 miles. The light was closed. It was first thought to be a trawler that was fishing but eventually was seen to be a hospital ship proceeding to the north-west.

The Italian hospital ship Sicilia (9646 GRT, built 1924) was supposed to sail at 10 knots at 2100 B/29 from Augusta for Valona. She may have sailed earlier than anticipated and perhaps the estimate that she was proceeding to the north-west was wrong. (21)

30 May 1941 At 2015 B/30, in position 37°21'N, 15°32'E, HMS Upright (Lt. E.D. Norman, DSC, RN) sighted the masts and the funnel of a merchant vessel bearing 130° proceeding on course of approximately 210°. The vessel was proceeding at fairly high speed. No attack was possible.
We have been unable to identify this vessel. (21)

31 May 1941 In a signal timed 0959 B/31, the following orders were signalled to HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. R.D. Cayley, RN) and HMS Upright (Lt. E.D. Norman, DSC, RN) by the Senior Officer Submarines Malta; Leave patrol as follows to arrive Marsamxett 0730 B/4. Utmost P.M./2 through position 36°15'N, 12°50'E. Upright P.M./3 through position 35°51'N, 15°15'E and 36°00'N, 14°52'E. (20)
4 Jun 1941 Around 0800 B/4, HMS Upright (Lt. E.D. Norman, DSC, RN) ended her 11th war patrol (9th in the Mediterranean) at Malta. (21)

15 Jun 1941 Around 1715 B/15, HMS Upright (Lt. J.S. Wraith, DSC, RN) departed Malta for her 12th war patrol (10th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol between the Kerkennah shallows and Lampion Island in area 'W', between latitudes 34°58'N, and 35°45'W and longitudes 11°02'E and 12°08'E.
On leaving harbour an attack exercise was carried out during which HMS Beryl (?) served as target. Later another practice attack was made on HMS Abingdon (Lt. G.A. Simmers, RNR). (21)
15 Jun 1941 For the daily positions of HMS Upright during her 12th war patrol see the map below.
17 Jun 1941 At 0330 B/17, HMS Upright (Lt. J.S. Wraith, DSC, RN) entered area 'W' and commenced to patrol to the east of No.1 buoy.

In a signal timed 1120 B/17 the Cdr. S.1 sent the following to HMS Upright; Reported crew may shortly be boarding the Italian transport Perseo (4857 GRT, built 1921) in position 3 miles east of No. 0 (?) buoy Kerkennah to effect salvage. Consider one visit to locality might be fruitful. Attack if possible.

At 1730 B/22, in position 36°38'N, 11°36'E, the Perseo been bombed by two Blenheims of 82 Squadron (RAF), and had one killed and ten wounded. The italian torpedo boat Enrico Cosenz, which had been sent to her assistance, rescued her crew, but the bad weather prevented her from towing Perseo away. The Italian tug Montecristo did not fare better, Perseo was drifting and finally was beached in shallow waters (8 metres) about 10 miles east of La Louza (nearly 30 miles north-east of Sfax). She was later raised and repaired. (22)

18 Jun 1941 HMS Upright (Lt. J.S. Wraith, DSC, RN) proceeded at periscope depth to close the position in which the Italian torpedo boat Perseo was reported to have been grounded, 3 miles to the east of No. 0 Kerkennah buoy. It had been reported salvage attempts were being undertaken.

At 1300 B/18, HMS Upright crossed the 10 fathom line. Lt. Wraith decided not to proceed any further in even shallower water. Course was altered to 080° to proceed along the 10 fathom line but northing was seen of the reported standed Italian torpedo boat. (21)

22 Jun 1941 In a signal timed 0907 B/22, the Senior Officer Submarines Malta informed HMS Union (Lt. R.M. Galloway, RN), HMS Unique (Lt. A.F. Collett, RN) and HMS Upright (Lt. J.S. Wraith, DSC, RN) that at 0805 B/22, an enemy convoy of six transports and three destroyers had been sighted in position 200° - Lampedusa - 15 nautical miles. [There is apparently a typo in the war diary summaries which says '000° - Lampedusa - 15 nautical miles'. HMS Unique's patrol report has the correct position as the convoy reached the latitude of Lampedusa at 1045 B/22.] The convoy was proceeding on course 020° a at 7 knots.

This must have referred to the convoy made up the transports Amsterdam (Italian, 10873 GRT, built 1921), Col di Lana (Italian, 5891 GRT, built 1926), Ernesto (Italian,7399 GRT, built 1914), Giulia (Italian, 5921 GRT, built 1926), Tembien (Italian, 5584 GRT, built 1914) and Wachtfels (German, 8467 GRT, built 1928). They were en-route from Tripoli (departed around 1500 B/21) to Naples and were escorted by the Italian destroyer Lanzerotto Malocello and the Italian torpedo boats Procione, Orsa and Pegaso.

The convoy came under air attack by six Blenheim bombers at 1205 B/22, Wachtfels and Tembien were hit by bombs. Procione towed Wachtfels towards Pantelleria and at 0700 B/23, ceded the tow to the tug Montecristo which brought her tot he island. In the meantime, Tembien had taken a heavy list and and some of the crew abandoned ship. Pegaso went to her assistance to recover the crew but in the meanwhile, Tembien had corrected the list and appeared to be able to proceed. At 1251 B/22, Pegaso was about to transfer the crew back on board when an Italian seaplane signalled a submarine 3000 metres from Tembien. Pegaso suspended the transfer operation and rushed to the attack, dropping depth charges at 1254 B/22. A large air bubble was seen and the submarine briefly emerged. At 1259 B/22, Pegaso dropped another pattern (no actual number mentioned in her report). Believing the enemy submarine seriously damaged, the torpedo boat resumed the transfer of the crew to Tembien.
In a signal timed 1109 B/22, the Cdr. S.1 ordered the three submarines listed above to return to Malta on 26 June. (23)

24 Jun 1941 At 1315 B/24, in approximate position 35°10'N, 11°30'E, HMS Upright (Lt. J.S. Wraith, DSC, RN) sighted a Vichy-French trawler sweeping down channel flying a large red flag from the foremast. She turned round opposite Kerkennah No.1 buoy and steamed back up the channel.

At 1312 B/24, the Cdr. S.1's signal timed 1055 B/24 was received which ordered HMS Upright to proceed to the east of Tripoli [exact position in which HMS Upright was to patrol is not known] to intercept an important Messina - Tripoli convoy.

This must have referred to the convoy made up of the troop transports Esperia (Italian, 11398 GRT, built 1920), Marco Polo (Italian, 12567 GRT, built 1912), Oceania (Italian, 19507 GRT, built 1933) and Neptunia (Italian, 19475 GRT, built 1932) escorted by the destroyers Aviere, Geniere, Vincenzo Gioberti and Antonio da Noli which was on passage from Naples (sailed at 0400 B/25) to Tripoli. The torpedo boat Calliope had joined from Messina to reinforce the escort. Due to the enemy air activity the convoy broke off their passage and diverted to Taranto. A distant cover force made up of the heavy cruisers Trieste, Gorizia and escorted by the destroyers Corazziere, Carabiniere and Ascari was also deployed.
At 2112 B/24, course was set to proceed with despatch to the ordered position.

At 2355 B/24, the Cdr. S.1's signal timed 2150 B/24 was received which contained new orders. [no further details available, but she seemed to have been ordered to patrol to the north-east of Tripoli.] (22)

25 Jun 1941 In a signal timed 1921 B/25, the Cdr. S.1 informed HMS Upright (Lt. J.S. Wraith, DSC, RN), HMS Unique (Lt. A.F. Collett, RN) and HMS Regent (Lt.Cdr. H.C. Browne, RN) of the following; Four large merchant ships in square formation with two destroyers 2 miles ahead two destroyers on either beam and one destroyer astern position 37°30'N, 15°35'E, course 160°, speed 10 knots at 1835 B/25.
[This referred to the convoy mentioned in yesterday's event.] (23)
26 Jun 1941 At 0410 B/26, HMS Upright (Lt. J.S. Wraith, DSC, RN) dived in position 33°34'N, 13°55'E and commended patrol on line 050° [presumably this was in accordance with her orders.]

At 2110 B/26, having sighted nothing all day, HMS Upright surfaced and set course for Malta in accordance with the Cdr. S.1's signal timed 1120 B/26. She was to proceed through position 35°36'N, 14°08'E on the way back to Malta. (21)
28 Jun 1941 At 0700 B/28, HMS Upright (Lt. J.S. Wraith, DSC, RN) ended her 12th war patrol (10th in the Mediterranean) at Malta.

At 2100 B/28, HMS Upright departed Malta for her 13th war patrol (11th war patrol in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol south of the Strait of Messina in the vicinity of position 37°52'N, 16°13'E (east-south-east of Cape Spartivento), to intercept possible Vichy-French reinforcements for Syria. HMS Upright was sent out again on short notice as she had just returned from a blank patrol and had a full outfit of torpedoes on board.

29 Jun 1941 Between 0850 B/29 and 1000 B/29, HMS Upright (Lt. J.S. Wraith, DSC, RN) which was still on passage to her patrol position and at time in approximate position 36°20'N, 15°30'E (to the south-east of Cape Passero, Sicily), when approximately 60 distant depth charges were heard to explode. This must have been the depth charging of HMS Urge (Lt. E.P. Tomkinson, RN) which had attacked an Italian cruiser force.

At 2334 B/29, in position 37°01'N, 15°48'E, a submarine was sighted bearing 020° at a range of only 1000 yards. Avoiding action was taken. A later investigation showed that HMS Union (Lt. R.M. Galloway, RN) was in this position at the time given. (21)
30 Jun 1941 At 0410 B/30, HMS Upright (Lt. J.S. Wraith, DSC, RN) dived in or near her patrol position, 37°52'N, 16°13'E to the east-south-east of Cape Spartivento.
She surfaced at 2130 B/30. Nothing had been sighted all day. (21)
1 Jul 1941 At 0400 B/1, HMS Upright (Lt. J.S. Wraith, DSC, RN) dived.
At 1015 B/1, three small merchant vessels of around 1500 to 2500 tons were observed proceeding down the coast keeping three miles from the shore.

These were the Italian armed merchant cruiser Egitto (3220 GRT, built 1929) escorting the German transports Bellona (1297 GRT, built 1929) and Tinos (2827 GRT, built 1914). At 2230 B/30 June, they had sailed from Crotone for Messina. Egitto reported to be off Roccella Ionica at 0650 B/1 (she met at this time the Italian armed merchant cruiser Città di Napoli escorting three steamers on an opposite course). At 0900 B/1, Egitto received a signal to proceed to Catania instead of Messina and go through a point 20 miles south of Cape Spartivento. The Egitto-convoy reached Catania at 1945 B/1.?
At 1309 B/1, the Senior Officer Submarines Malta's signal timed 1109 B/1 was received.
At 2110 B/1, HMS Upright surfaced. Visibility had been poor throughout the day. Course was set to return to Malta as had been ordered by the Senior Officer Submarines Malta. (21)
3 Jul 1941 Around 0730 B/3, HMS Upright (Lt. J.S. Wraith, DSC, RN) ended her 13th war patrol (11th in the Mediterranean) at Malta. (21)

17 Jul 1941 In the afternoon, HMS Upholder (Lt.Cdr. M.D. Wanklyn, RN), HMS Upright (Lt. J.S. Wraith, DSC, RN), HMS Urge (Lt. E.P. Tomkinson, RN) and HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. R.D. Cayley, RN) conducted exercises off Malta with HMS Beryl (?). (24)

20 Jul 1941 Around 2000 B/20, HMS Upright (Lt. J.S. Wraith, DSC, RN) departed Malta for her 14th war patrol (12th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol between Capo Dell Armi and Capo Spartivento off the south coast of Calabria, Italy, in the vicinity of position 37°25'N, 15°47'E, to give cover for convoy operation Substance.
Passage through the Straits of Gibraltar of the eastbound convoy and sailing from Gibraltar of the remaining ships involved in the operation.

Around 0130 B/21 convoy WS 9C passed the Straits of Gibraltar. The convoy at that moment consisted of six merchant ships; City of Pretoria (8049 GRT, built 1937), Deucalion (7516 GRT, built 1930), Durham (10893 GRT, built 1934), Melbourne Star (11076 GRT, built 1936), Port Chalmers (8535 GRT, built 1933) and Sydney Star (11095 GRT, built 1936).

At the time they passed through the Straits they were escorted by HMS Nelson (Capt. T.H. Troubridge, RN), HMS Edinburgh (Capt. H.W. Faulkner, RN, flying the flag of Rear-Admiral E.N. Syfret, RN), HMS Manxman (Capt. R.K. Dickson, RN), HMS Lightning (Cdr. R.G. Stewart, RN), HMAS Nestor (Cdr. A.S. Rosenthal, RAN), HMS Avon Vale (Lt.Cdr. P.A.R. Withers, RN), HMS Eridge (Lt.Cdr. W.F.N. Gregory-Smith, RN) and HMS Farndale (Cdr. S.H. Carlill, RN).

HMS Manchester (Capt. H. Drew, DSC, RN), HMS Arethusa (Capt. A.C. Chapman, RN), HMS Cossack (Capt. E.L. Berthon, DSC and Bar, RN), HMS Maori (Cdr. R.E. Courage, DSO, DSC and Bar, RN), HMS Sikh (Cdr. G.H. Stokes, RN) departed Gibraltar around 0200 B/21 escorting troopship Leinster (4302 GRT, built 1937) which was to join the convoy. However Leinster grounded while leaving Gibraltar and had to left behind. The small fleet tanker RFA Brown Ranger (3417 GRT, built 1941, master D.B.C. Ralph) left Gibraltar around the same time escorted by the destroyer HMS Beverley (Lt.Cdr. J. Grant, RN).

About one hour later, around 0300 B/21, HMS Renown (Rear-Admiral R.R. McGrigor, RN, flying the flag of Vice-Admiral J.F. Somerville, KCB, DSO, RN), HMS Ark Royal (Capt. L.E.H. Maund, RN), HMS Hermione (Capt. G.N. Oliver, RN), HMS Faulknor (Capt. A.F. de Salis, RN), HMS Fearless (Cdr. A.F. Pugsley, RN), HMS Firedrake (Lt.Cdr. S.H. Norris, DSO, DSC, RN), HMS Foresight (Cdr. J.S.C. Salter, RN), HMS Forester (Lt.Cdr. E.B. Tancock, DSC and Bar, RN), HMS Foxhound (Cdr. G.H. Peters, DSC, RN), HMS Fury (Lt.Cdr. T.C. Robinson, RN) and HMS Duncan (Lt.Cdr. A.N. Rowell, RN) departed Gibraltar to give convoy for the convoy during the passage to Malta.
At sea the forces were redistributed; Force H, the cover force HMS Renown, HMS Nelson, HMS Ark Royal, HMS Hermione, HMS Faulknor, HMS Foresight, HMS Forester, HMS Fury, HMS Lightning and HMS Duncan.

Force X, the close escort for the convoy HMS Edinburgh, HMS Manchester, HMS Arethusa, HMS Manxman, HMS Cossack, HMS Maori, HMS Sikh, HMAS Nestor, HMS Fearless, HMS Firedrake, HMS Foxhound, HMS Avon Vale, HMS Eridge and HMS Farndale.
Plan for the operation

Force H was to cover the convoy until it reached the narrows between Sicily and Tunisia. Force X was to escort the convoy all the way to Malta. Ships of Force X also had troops for Malta on board that had been taken to Gibraltar by troopship Pasteur. On 23 July 1941, the day the eastbound convoy would reach ‘the narrows’ five empty transports and two tankers would depart Malta for Gibraltar (Convoy MG 1) The seven empty transports were; Group 1 (speed 17 knots) HMS Breconshire (9776 GRT, built 1939), Talabot (6798 GRT, built 1936),
Group 2 (speed 14 knots) Thermopylae (6655 GRT, built 1930), Amerika (10218 GRT, built 1930),

Group 3 (speed 12 knots) Settler (6202 GRT, built 1939), Tanker Svenor (7616 GRT, built 1931) and Tanker Hoegh Hood (9351 GRT, built 1936) These were escorted by the destroyer HMS Encounter (Lt.Cdr. E.V.St J. Morgan, RN) which had been repairing and refitting at Malta.

Through intelligence it was known that the Italian Navy had five battleships operational (three of them at Taranto) and about ten cruisers divided between Taranto, Palermo and Messina. The Italian Air Force had about 50 torpedo planes and 150 bombers (30 of which were dive bombers) stationed in Sardinia and Sicily, roughly half of each type on both islands.

The Royal Air Force was able to be of more help than during the previous convoy trip from Gibraltar to Malta last January. Aircraft from Gibraltar conducted A/S patrols for the fleet during the first two days of the passage to the east. Also patrols were flown between Sardinia and the coast of Africa, while aircraft from Malta conducted reconnaissance between Sardinia and Sicily, besides watching the Italian ports. Malta would also provide fighter escort for Force X and the convoy after Force H would part with them and HMS Ark Royal could no longer provide fighter cover for them.

During the operation eight submarines (HMS Olympus (Lt.Cdr. H.G. Dymott, RN), HMS Unique (Lt. A.F. Collett, RN), HMS Upholder (Lt.Cdr. M.D. Wanklyn, RN), HMS Upright (Lt. J.S. Wraith, DSC, RN), HMS Urge (Lt. E.P. Tomkinson, RN), HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. R.D. Cayley, DSO, RN), HMS P 32 (Lt. D.A.B. Abdy, RN) and HrMs O 21 (Lt.Cdr. J.F. van Dulm, RNN)) were on patrol to report and attack Italian warships that might be sailed to intercept the convoy.
The passage East, 22 July 1941

On 22 July the destroyers from Force X oiled from the Brown Ranger two at a time. A task that took about 10 hours. Having completed the oiling of the destroyers the Brown Ranger and her escort returned to Gibraltar. An Italian aircraft had reported Force H in the morning but the convoy and Force X, at that moment about 100 nautical miles to the south-westward, appeared not to have been sighed. At 2317 B/22 the Italian submarine Diaspro missed HMS Renown with torpedoes. HMAS Nestor sighted the torpedo tracks and was able to warn HMS Renown which was then able to avoid the torpedoes by doing an emergency turn to port.
The passage East and attacks by the Italian Air Force, 23 July 1941

Force H rejoined the convoy around 0800 B/23 as the British were now approaching the danger area. Shadowing aircraft had already reported the position of the fleet that morning and heavy air attacks soon followed.

The first came at 0945 hours, a well times combination of nine high level bombers and six or seven torpedo planes approaching from the north-east. HMS Ark Royal had eleven fighters up, which met the bombers about 20 miles from the fleet. They managed to down two of the nine bombers but unfortunately three Fulmars were shot down by the enemy. The other seven bombers came on working round the head of the screen of destroyers to attack the convoy from the starboard beam at a height of 10000 feet. Their bombs fell harmlessly amongst the leading ships as they altered course to avoid the attack. The torpedo planes however were more successful. They came from ahead out of the sun, flying low, and as the destroyers opened fire they divided into groups of two or three and to attack the convoy on both sides. Two aircraft attacked HMS Fearless, stationed ahead in the screen, dropping their torpedoes at ranges of 1500 and 800 yards from a height of 70 feet. The destroyer avoided the first torpedo, but was hit by the second, set on fire, and completely disabled. Other aircraft went to press on their attacks on the convoy itself. One of them, dropping its torpedo between two merchant vessels hit HMS Manchester as she was turning to regain her station after avoiding two torpedoes fired earlier. She reversed helm once more but to no avail. During the attacks three enemy torpedo bombers were shot down by AA fire from the ships.

HMS Manchester was badly damaged and could only use one engine out of four. At first she could steam only 8 knots. She was ordered to make for Gibraltar with HMS Avon Vale as escort. That evening, further to the westward, they were attacked again by three enemy torpedo planes but their AA gunfire kept the enemy at a distance. Both ships successfully reached Gibraltar on the 26th.

At 1010 B/23 five more bombers tried to attack the convoy crossing this time from north to south. Fighters from HMS Ark Royal forced them to drop their bombs from great height and mostly outside the screen.
At 1645 B/23 five more torpedo planes led by a seaplane came in from the northward. Three Fulmars caught them about 20 miles away. They managed to shoot down two planes and drove the remainder away.

Soon afterwards the fleet arrived off the entrance to the Skerki Channel. There HMS Hermione was transferred to Force X to take the place of HMS Manchester. Six destroyers were assigned to Force H and eight to Force X. At 1713 hours Vice-Admiral Somerville hauled round to the westward. HMS Ark Royal kept her Fulmars up until RAF Beaufighters had arrived from Malta to take over.

The convoy was attacked again around 1900 B/23. Four torpedo planes arrived from the eastward, flying low and and working round from ahead to the starboard side of the convoy. They approached in pairs in line abreast. They kept HMS Sikh (on the starboard bow of the screen) between them and their target until nearly the moment for attack, thereby hampering the AA fire from the other ships. They dropped their torpedoes from long range from a height of 50 feet and nearly hit HMS Hermione, sternmost ship in the starboard column. To avoid the attack each column of the convoy turned 90° outwards and all warships opened barrage fire from all guns that would bear. The barrage however fell short but it caused the Italians to drop their torpedoes early. Also one of the enemy was possibly shot down.
