This attack scattered the convoy and it took some time to reform. At 1945 B/23 about seven bombers appeared from ahead at a height of about 14000 feet to attack the convoy from the port side. The convoy altered 40° to port together and the escort opened up a controlled fire with some hesitation as the Italian aircraft looked a lot like Beaufighters. The bombing was extremely accurate. Several bombs fell near HMS Edinburgh which was leading the port column, and a near miss abreast a boiler room disabled HMS Firedrake which had been sweeping ahead of the convoy. She could no longer steam so Rear-Admiral Syfret ordered her back to Gibraltar in tow of HMS Eridge. They had an anxious passage, being shadowed by aircraft continuously during daylight hours, but were not again attacked. On the 25th HMS Firedrake managed to lit one boiler so the tow was slipped. Both destroyers entered Gibraltar harbour on the 27th.

Soon after leaving the Skerki Channel in the evening of the 23th the convoy hauled up to the north-east towards the coast of Sicily. This was to lessen the danger of mines. The Italians did not shadow the convoy after the attack at 1945 hours and missed this alteration of course which they clearly did not expect. Around 2100 hours, as it was getting dark, enemy aircraft were seen searching along its old line of advance. During the evening the convoy sighted flares several times about 20 miles to the south.
Continued passage to the east and enemy attacks, 24 July 1941

Between 0250 and 0315 hours the convoy was however attacked by the Italian MAS boats MAS 532 and MAS 533. The managed to torpedo and damaged the Sydney Star. HMAS Nestor went alongside and took off almost 500 soldiers. Sydney Star was however able to continue her passage as staggler escorted initially by HMAS Nestor. Admiral Syfret however sent back HMS Hermione. At 1000 B/24 eight German dive bombers and two high level bombers attacked. Their bombs fell close the escorting ships. HMS Hermione shot down one dive bomber. The three ships arrived at Malta early in the afternoon.

The main body of the convoy meanwhile continued on its way unhindered after the attacks of the motor torpedo boats except for an attempt by three torpedo planes around 0700 hours. They dropped their torpedoes at a safe distance when fired on by the destroyers in the screen ahead. According to the orders Rear-Admiral Syfret was to leave the convoy now, if there was no threat from Italian surface forces, and go on to Malta with the cruisers and some of the destroyers. They were to land the passengers and stores, complete with fuel and return to Force H as soon as possible. The remaining destroyers were to accompany the transports to Malta. They too were to join Force H as soon as possible. Rear-Admiral Syfret felt easy about the surface danger as all Italian ships were reported in harbour the day before, but he was anxious about the threat to the convoy from the air. He decided to go ahead with the cruiser but leave all destroyers with the convoy so at 0745 B/24, HMS Edinburgh, HMS Arethusa and HMS Manxman left the convoy and pressed ahead at high speed to Malta where they arrived at noon the same day. The transports and the destroyers arrived about four hours later. They had been attacked only once by a torpedo plane since the cruisers separated.
Return passage of the warships of force X to make rendez-vous with Force H.

In the evening HMS Edinburgh, HMS Arethusa, HMS Hermione and HMS Manxman sailed together followed by five destroyers; HMS Cossack, HMS Maori, HMS Sikh, HMAS Nestor, HMS Foxhound, later the same evening. The destroyers overtook the cruisers in the morning of the 25th. The sixth destroyer, HMS Farndale, had to be left at Malta due to defects (condenser problems). All ships made rendez-vous with Force H to the north-west of Galita Island at 0800 B/25.
Movements of Force H after it parted from the convoy.

After parting with the convoy in the evening of the 23rd, Vice-Admiral Somerville had taken force H westward at 18 knots until the afternoon of the 24th going as far west as 03°30’E. He then turned back to meet Admiral Syfret, also sending from HMS Ark Royal six Swordfish aircraft which left her in position 37°42’N, 07°17’E at 1000 B/25. After their junction Forces H and X made the best of way towards Gibraltar. Fighter patrols of HMS Ark Royal shot down a shadowing aircraft soon after the fleet had shaped course to the westward, losing a Fulmar in doing so. However another aircraft had meanwhile reported the fleet.

High level bombers appeared from the east and torpedo bombers from the north at 1100 hours. HMS Ark Royal at that moment had four fighters in the air and sent up six more. They prevented the bombing attack shooting down three aircraft out of eight at a cost of two Fulmars, while the ships watched the enemy jettison their bombs 15 miles away. The torpedo attack came to nothing too for the enemy gave up the attempt and retired while still several miles from the fleet. Two days later, on the 27th, the fleet reached Gibraltar.
The movements of the seven empty ships coming from Malta.

Six of the transports / tankers left Malta for Gibraltar in the morning of the 23rd, escorted by HMS Encounter. The seventh ship, tanker Svenor grounded while leaving harbour and was held up for some hours. At dusk, when a few miles from Pantelleria, the six ships devided into pairs according to their speed. HMS Encounter initially escorted the middle pair but joined the leading ships in the evening of the 24th when past the Galita Bank.

Italian aircraft, both high level bombers and torpedo planes, attacked all these ships on the 24th to the southward of Sardinia. They made their first attempt on the second pair of transports and HMS Encounter. Four torpedo planes attacked at 1230 B/24 and four bombers at 1250 B/24. No ships were hit though the bombs fell close. Next came the turn for the leading pair, which were attacked further westwards by two bombers that came singly at 1330 B/24 and 1400 B/24. The second plane nearly hit HMS Breconshire. Finally when the third pair of ships reached about the same position in the evening they were attacked by torpedo planes and the Hoegh Hood was damaged but she managed to arrive at Gibraltar only a few hours after her consort on the 27th. The last ship, the one that had been delayed at Malta, arrived on the 28th. (25)
22 Jul 1941 At 0420 B/22, HMS Upright (Lt. J.S. Wraith, DSC, RN) dived in her patrol position (37°25'N, 15°47'E) and patrolled east and west at 4 miles from the land.
No coastal traffic at all was encountered. Enemy air activity along the coast was slight. (21)

23 Jul 1941 At 0820 B/23, in approximate position 37°47'N, 15°49'E, HMS Upright (Lt. J.S. Wraith, DSC, RN) sighted two steamers of 2000 to 3000 tons escorted by an armed merchant cruiser of about 5000 tons. They were steering a westerly course along the coast one mile out. The small steamers were steering a steady course of 265° and the armed merchant cruiser was following astern, zigging 70° either side of the mean course. An attempt was made to close the range and attack the armed merchant cruiser but the range could not be brought below 5000 yards and the attack was broken off. The convoy hugged the coast the whole way. The convoy was seen to turn northwards making for Messina after rounding Cape dell'Armi.

This convoy was made up of the German transport Tinos (2827 GRT, built 1914) and Italian transport Bolsena (2384 GRT, built 1918) screened by the Italian armed merchant cruiser Egitto (3329 GRT, built 1928). They sailed from Crotone at 1915 B/22, speed 9 knots, Tinos for Reggio (Calabria) and Bolsena for Catania. At 1007 B/23, they were off Punta Pellaro and at 1043 B/23, Egitto ordered Tinos to enter Reggio while Bolsena followed her to Catania where they arrived at 1615 B/23.

At 1000 B/23, a large hospital ship of 10000 to 12000 tons was seen leaving the Straits of Messina. She appeared to be escorted out of the Straits by a small escort vessel which turned back when the hospital ship set off on a course of 190°. It is thought the hospital ship was the Italian hospital ship Gradisca (13868 GRT, built 1913).

This was however the Vichy-French hospital ship Canada (9684 GRT, built 1912) escorted through the Straits of Messina by an (unidentified) Italian vessel (they were sighted by the armed merchant cruiser Egitto at 1000 B/23). This escort was probably an auxiliary vessel. Unfortunately, the Messina command files makes no mention of the passage of Canada. (21)
24 Jul 1941 HMS Upright (Lt. J.S. Wraith, DSC, RN) fires two torpedoes at the Italian floating dry-dock GO 22 off Cape dell'Armi near position 37°52'N, 15°50'E.

This dock was towed by the tugs Teseo and Volante and escorted by the torpedo boats Aretusa and Rosolino Pilo. The torpedoes miss their target (although two hits were claimed) but one hits the towing wire and the floating dock is slightly damaged. Upright is heavily depth charged by the two escorting torpedo boats. Later the motor torpedo boat MAS 524 sails from Messina to join the hunt and drops an additional six depth charges.

1010 B/24 - Sighted an auxiliary patrol vessel (converted yacht) steering a westerly course about one mile from the shore. There were constant air patrols along the coast so some traffic may be expected.
1115 B/24 - Sighted masts and funnels of two destroyers rounding Cape Spartivento. Two aircraft were seen patrolling down the coast. Looked like something might come our way.
1120 B/24 - Sighted masts and funnels of one more destroyer and two other vessels.
1122 B/24 - Sighted a long low vessel that looked like an aircraft carrier.
1130 B/24 - The long low vessel was now identified as a dry dock that was being towed by two tugs and escorted by three destroyers and two aircraft. Started attack.
1237 B/24 - Fired three torpedoes (four were intended, but one torpedo misfired) from 1900 yards. Two loud explosion and one smaller explosion followed. It was thought two hits were obtained.

1241 B/24 - The first pattern of five depth charges was dropped followed by more attacks that were rather close. No damage was done to Upright but she lost trim and ended up at 210 feet (150 feet was intended). She could only be controlled by blowing main ballast something Lt. Wraith rather did not want to do with three destroyers hunting overhead.

1315 B/24 - The depth charges were now more distant and astern. Meanwhile Upright had sunk to 340 feet and again main ballast had to be blown. The result was that the destroyers came nearer again and started dropping more depth charges again rather close. No damage was caused by the depth charges but minor damage was caused by the great depth Upright had ended up.

1415 B/24 - No more depth charges had been dropped now for almost one hour and HE was astern. Upright was now at 200 feet. The destroyers remained in the area during the whole afternoon but dropped no more depth charges. HMS Upright only surfaced at 2200 B/24. (21)
27 Jul 1941 At 0020 B/27, HMS Upright (Lt. J.S. Wraith, DSC, RN) passed a signal to the Senior Officer Submarines Malta that she was returning to Malta due to leaks and electrical defects. (21)
28 Jul 1941 Around 0730 B/28, HMS Upright (Lt. J.S. Wraith, DSC, RN) ended her 14th war patrol (12th in the Mediterranean) at Malta. (21)
29 Jul 1941 HMS Upright (Lt. J.S. Wraith, DSC, RN) is docked in No.2 graving dock at the Malta Dockyard. (26)
1 Aug 1941 HMS Upright (Lt. J.S. Wraith, DSC, RN) is undocked. (27)
8 Aug 1941 In the afternoon HMS Upright (Lt. J.S. Wraith, DSC, RN) conducted a dive test off Malta. The results were unsatisfactory. (27)
22 Aug 1941 In the morning HMS Upright (Lt. J.S. Wraith, DSC, RN) conducted a dive test off Malta. The results were satisfactory. (27)

24 Aug 1941 Around 1600 B/24, HMS Upright (Lt. J.S. Wraith, DSC, RN) departed Malta for her 15th war patrol (13th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol off Marettimo Island between latitudes 38°00'N, and 39°00'N and longitudes 11°00'E and 12°36'E.
On leaving Malta she was to proceed through position 36°30'N, 13°40'E and pass submerged through Q.B.B. 255 [mined zone] during daytime on the 25th.
On departure gunnery exercises were carried out on a target that was being towed by the tug St. Angelo. (21)
24 Aug 1941 For the daily positions of HMS Upright during her 15th war patrol see the map below.

25 Aug 1941 In a signal timed 2110 B/25, the Senior Officer Submarines Malta sent the following to HMS Upright (Lt. J.S. Wraith, DSC, RN); Enemy unit not located by afternoon by air reconnaissance from Marittimo to the south of Sardinia. They may be returning to base. Upright was to proceed to position 38°27'N, 12°40'E. (28)
26 Aug 1941 At 0510 B/26, HMS Upright (Lt. J.S. Wraith, DSC, RN) dived 15 miles short of the ordered position (38°27'N, 12°40'E). The ordered position was finally reached at 0915 B/26.

In a signal timed 1025 B/26, HMS Upright the Senior Officer Submarines Malta informed HMS Upright that at 0912 B/26 an enemy cruiser had been sighted to the east-south-east of Cagliari in position 38°54'N, 10°00'E. Enemy course was 100° at a speed of 20 knots. This signal was received at 1152 B/26.

At 1520 B/26, an Oriani-class destroyer was sighted steering on course 065° at 20 knots. She passed 12 miles to the north-west of Cape St. Vito. An attempt was made close range and attack but the range was never less than 7000 yards. The attack was broken off at 1535 B/26. It was considered that this destroyer must have been the cruiser that had been reported in the Senior Officer Submarines Malta's signal timed 1025 B/26.
This was the Italian destroyer Scirocco which had sailed from Cagliari at 0550 B/26 and arrived at Palermo at 1831 B/26.
At 1600 B/26, HMS Upright resumed patrol off Cape St. Vito.
At 2200 B/26, course was altered to proceed to the area given in the Senior Officer Submarines Malta signal timed 1245 B/26 (see below). This area was patrolled during the night. (21)

26 Aug 1941 In a signal timed 1245 B/26, the Senior Officer Submarines Malta, informed HMS Upright (Lt. J.S. Wraith, DSC, RN) and HMS Urge (Lt. E.P. Tomkinson, RN) that an 8 knot southbound convoy escorted by a destroyer and a torpedo boat was expected off Marittimo about 0500 B/27.

HMS Upright was to intercept in position 38°43'N, 12°35'E at about 2000 B/26. HMS Urge was to patrol between position ' L' [38°16'N, 12°08'E] and Marittimo until 0800 B/27 then to carry out original programme. HMS Upright was to remain east of 012°10'E between 38°20'N and 38°30'N until HMS Urge and HMS Triumph (Cdr. W.J.W. Woods, RN) had passed [HMS Triumph was already off the north-coast of Sicily though].

The convoy referred to was on passage from Naples to Tripoli and was made up of the transports Aquitania (Italian, 4971 GRT, built 1924), Col di Lana (Italian, 5891 GRT, built 1926), Ernesto (Italian, 7399 GRT, built 1914), Riv (Italian, 6650 GRT, built 1921) and the tanker Poza Rica (Italian, 7799 GRT, built 1940). It was escorted by the destroyers Euro, Alfredo Oriani and the torpedo boats Procione, Orsa and Clio.
This convoy had sailed from Naples at 0245 B/26 - 0500 B/26 and reached Tripoli around 1300 B/29. It was reinforced by the torpedo boat Pegaso from Palermo at 0600 B/27. (28)

27 Aug 1941 At 0700 B/27, while dived about 8 to 10 miles from the Sicilian coast to the south-west of Cape St. Vito, HMS Upright (Lt. J.S. Wraith, DSC, RN) heard distant explosions. This was HMS Urge attacking the reported convoy [see the event of the 26th or the page of HMS Urge for the composition of this convoy]. (21)
28 Aug 1941 In a signal timed 1711 B/28, the Senior Officer Submarines Malta, informed HMS Upright (Lt. J.S. Wraith, DSC, RN) and HMS Urge (Lt. E.P. Tomkinson, RN) of the following.
A 17-knot (troop) convoy including the Italian troop transport (liner) Oceania (19507 GRT, built 1933) leaves Naples probably late P.M./29 arriving Marittimo area mid-day/30.
HMS Urge remain in the Naples vicinity north of 40°N until dawn/30 then to the south-east according to programme.

HMS Upright patrol in the vicinity of position 38°07'N, 12°00'E until fast convoy passes or until 2359 B/30, then move to vicinity of position 38°09'N, 12°36'E. 12-knot northbound convoy probably due Marittimo about 2000 B/31 and Naples mid-day /1 (September). Information is rather vague.

The fast troop convoy from Naples to Tripoli, referred to was made up of the troop transports Oceania (19507 GRT, built 1933), Neptunia (19475 GRT, built 1932) and Victoria (13098 GRT, built 1931) escorted by the destroyers Aviere, Camicia Nera, Vincenzo Gioberti, Antonio da Noli, Antoniotto Usodimare and Emanuele Pessagno. On leaving Naples on the 29th it was attacked by HMS Urge. The Oceania-convoy sailed from Naples at 0910 B/29 - 1100 B/29 and reached Tripoli at 0940 B/31 - 1100 B/31.

The other, northbound, convoy (Tripoli-Trapani/Naples) referred to was made up the Italian transports Caffaro (6476 GRT, built 1924), Giulia (5921 GRT, built 1926), Marin Sanudo (5958 GRT, built 1926), Nicolo Odero (6003 GRT, built 1925) and the Italian tanker Minatitlán (7651 GRT, built 1941). The Italian auxiliary magnetic minesweeper Eritrea (2517 GRT, built 1918) was also part of this convoy which was escorted by the Italian destroyers Alfredo Oriani, Euro and the Italian torpedo boats Calliope, Orsa and Pegaso. The Caffaro-convoy sailed from Tripoli at 1745 B29 - 1835 B/29 and reached Naples at 1240 B - 1435 B/1. (28)

29 Aug 1941 At 0520 B/29, while patrolling about 15 miles to the north-west of Cape St. Vito, HMS Upright (Lt. J.S. Wraith, DSC, RN) picked up faint HE which appeared to be be from a destroyer. This HE died away quickly on a north-easterly bearing. Nothing further was seen or heard all day.
[No destroyer or torpedo boat was in this area at this time though.] (21)

30 Aug 1941 In a signal timed 1013 B/30, the Senior Officer Submarines Malta informed HMS Upright (Lt. J.S. Wraith, DSC, RN) and HMS Triumph (Cdr. W.J.W. Woods, RN) of the following; Six merchant ships and five destroyers, speed 9 knots, off Kerkennah steering northward at 0900 B/30.

This was the convoy made up of the Italian transports Caffaro (6476 GRT, built 1924), Giulia (5921 GRT, built 1926), Marin Sanudo (5958 GRT, built 1926), Nicolo Odero (6003 GRT, built 1925) and the Italian tanker Minatitlán (7651 GRT, built 1941). The Italian auxiliary magnetic minesweeper Eritrea (2517 GRT, built 1918) was also part of this convoy which was escorted by the Italian destroyers Alfredo Oriani, Euro and the Italian torpedo boats Calliope, Orsa and Pegaso. This convoy sailed from Tripoli at 1745 B29 - 1835 B/29 and reached Naples at 1240 B - 1435 B/1. (28)

30 Aug 1941 In a signal timed 1629 B/30, the Senior Officer Submarines Malta ordered HMS Upright (Lt. J.S. Wraith, DSC, RN) to proceed to position 38°09'N, 12°36'E (west of Cape St. Vito) to intercept the convoy reported in his signals timed 1711 B/28 and 1013 B/30. The signal was received at 1758 B/30 and course was immediately set to 090° to comply with these orders. (29)
31 Aug 1941 At 0500 B/31, HMS Upright (Lt. J.S. Wraith, DSC, RN) dived 5 miles to the west of the ordered position and continued on course 090°.
Nothing was seen or heard all day of the expected convoy though. (21)
1 Sep 1941 At 1200 B/1, HMS Upright (Lt. J.S. Wraith, DSC, RN) left patrol near Cape St. Vito to return to Malta. Course was set to pass north of and west of Marittimo Island. (21)
2 Sep 1941 During daylight hours HMS Upright (Lt. J.S. Wraith, DSC, RN) made the submerged passage of Q.B.B. 255. (21)
3 Sep 1941 Around 1400 B/3, HMS Upright (Lt. J.S. Wraith, DSC, RN) ended her 15th war patrol (13th in the Mediterranean) at Malta. (21)
10 Sep 1941 In the afternoon HMS Upright (Lt. J.S. Wraith, DSC, RN) conducted attack exercises off Malta during which HMS Abingdon (Lt. G.A. Simmers, RNR) served as target. (30)

16 Sep 1941 HMS Upright (Lt. J.S. Wraith, DSC, RN) departed Malta for her 16th war patrol (14th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol the approaches to Tripoli, Libya to intercept an important enemy troop convoy together with HMS Unbeaten (Lt.Cdr. E.A. Woodward, RN), HMS Upholder (Lt.Cdr. M.D. Wanklyn, DSO, RN) and HMS Ursula (Lt. A.R. Hezlet, RN).
HMS Upright was to patrol in position 33°01'N, 14°42’E.

The convoy in question was made up of the Italian troop transports Oceania (19507 GRT, built 1933), Neptunia (19475 GRT, built 1932) and Vulcania (24469 GRT, built 1928) which were on passage from Taranto to Tripoli. They were escorted by the destroyers Nicoloso da Recco, Antonio da Noli, Antoniotto Usodimare and Emanuele Pessagno, Vincenzo Gioberti and Alfredo Oriani.

18 Sep 1941 In the early hours of this day, HMS Upright (Lt. J.S. Wraith, DSC, RN) arrived in her ordered patrol position, 33°01'N, 14°42'E. She commenced to patrol on a north - south line turning 2 miles either side of the patrol position.
At 0331 B/18, an enemy report (time of origin 0320 B/18) from HMS Unbeaten (Lt.Cdr. E.A. Woodward, RN) was received reporting the convoy passing 5 miles to the northward of her position.
At 0335 B/18, course was altered to 180° and increased speed to 11 knots to try to intercept.
At 0430 B/18, course was altered to course 000° and HMS Upright commenced to patrol across the expected path of the convoy as had been reported in the contact report of HMS Unbeaten timed 0320 B/18.
At 0459 B/18, a contact report timed 0419 B/18 from HMS Upholder (Lt.Cdr. M.D. Wanklyn, DSO, RN) was received. She reported two damaged merchant vessels in position 33°01'N, 14°51'E.
At 0505 B/18, course was altered to 020° and speed increased to 11 knots to investigate this report.
At 0508 B/18, an object was sighted ahead at long range. It was burning lights. Started to close to attack.
At 0515 B/18, a destroyer was sighted on the starboard bow at a range of 5 to 6 miles.

At 0525 B/18, a second destroyer was sighted. By now the first object sighted at 0508 B/18 was also identified as a destroyer which was stopped with lights burning. It was now too light to remain on the surface so HMS Upright dived. The range to the nearest destroyer was 6000 yards.

At 0530 B/18, the three destroyers commenced an A/S hunt. A few depth charges were dropped but it was not considered likely that HMS Upright had been sighted when still on the surface. She may have been heard diving though.

Up to 1100 B/18, the enemy was hunting in the area. Once a destroyer, of the Navigatori-class, passed at a range of 1000 yards on a steady course at 24 knots. An attack could have been carried out but the torpedo tubes were all loaded with Mk. IV torpedoes set to 18 feet as had been ordered by the Capt. S.10.
At 0930 B/18, course was altered to 355° to proceed to position 33°37'N, 14°31'E as had been ordered in the Capt. S.10's in an earlier signal.
At 0958 B/18, two distant explosions were heard.
At 1240 B/18, more distant explosions were heard.
At 1535 B/18, two distant explosions, thought to be depth charges, were heard. The were followed by another, louder, explosion.
At 2200 B/18, HMS Upright arrived in position 33°37'N, 14°31'E. She commenced to patrol north and south of this position.
At 2322 B/18, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 2140 B/18 was received which ordered HMS Upright to return to Malta. Course was set to 355°, speed 8 knots to comply with the orders in this signal. (21)
20 Sep 1941 At 0640 B/20, HMS Upright (Lt. J.S. Wraith, DSC, RN) ended her 16th war patrol (14th in the Mediterranean) at Malta. (21)

20 Sep 1941 In a signal timed 1559 A/20, the Vice Admiral Malta sent the following orders to HMS Upright (Lt.Cdr. J.H. Eaden, DSC, RN); Leave 34°N, 13°E and pass through positions 35°00'N, 14°00'E, 35°35'N, 14°10'E to arrive off Grand Harbour at 0700 A/24. Delimara light will be exhibited from 0200 A/24. (31)

22 Sep 1941 Around 1600 B/22, HMS Upright (Lt. J.S. Wraith, DSC, RN) departed Malta for her 17th war patrol (15th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol to the north of the Straits of Messina.
At 2110 B/22, about 12 miles to the north-west of Gozo Island, Malta, a dark shape was sighted. This was thought to be possibly an enemy motor torpedo boat. Avoiding action was taken.
23 Sep 1941 During daylight hours, HMS Upright (Lt. J.S. Wraith, DSC, RN) made the submerged passage through Q.B.B. 255 [mined zone]. (21)

24 Sep 1941 At 0750 B/24, in approximate position 38°17'N, 12°47'E, HMS Upright (Lt. J.S. Wraith, DSC, RN) sighted a camouflaged merchant vessel steering eastward. When 5 miles were of Cape St. Vito this ship turned south presumably going to Trapani.
[We have been unable to identify this vessel.]

At 1450 B/24, when in approximate position 38°21'N, 13°02'E, the mast, funnel and bridge of a merchant vessel was sighted to the southward, fairly close inshore. This vessel was lost from sight 15 minutes later.
[We have also been unable to identify this vessel.]
At 1715 B/24, an explosion, thought to be possibly a torpedo, was heard.
At 2359 A/24, HMS Upright entered her patrol area. (21)

25 Sep 1941 At 1510 B/25, near Cape Milazzo, in approximate position 38°20'N, 15°10'E, HMS Upright (Lt. J.S. Wraith, DSC, RN) sighted a small coastal steamer of about 800 tons to the southward. The enemy was proceeding westwards.
Perhaps this was the Italian steamer Rubicone (4965 GRT, built 1906) which sailed from Messina at 1040 B/25 at 8.5 knots for Trapani (although the size is not a match).
At 1855 B/25, six distant explosions were heard at intervals. (21)

26 Sep 1941 At 0700 B/26, while north of Cape Rasocolmo, HMS Upright (Lt. J.S. Wraith, DSC, RN) sighted a torpedo boat at a range of 4 to 5 miles carrying out an A/S sweep. Went to 80 feet and came up to periscope depth every half hour to have a look around.
This was most probably the submarine chaser Albatros which sailed from Messina for an A/S sweep at 1900 B/25 and returned at 1500 B/26,

At 1130 B/26, HMS Upright sighted two motor drifters and also a larger steam vessel of about 600 tons. The were minesweeping near Cape Rasocolmo proceeding westwards. They turned 180° to return to Messina at 1215 B/26.
At 1330 B/26, HMS Upright sighted a motor torpedo boat laying stopped about 2 miles away.
At 1430 B/26, HMS Upright sighted a motor torpedo boat on the port beam, 3 miles away.
At 1610 B/26, HMS Upright sighted a motor torpedo boat 2 miles away. Went to 70 feet.
At 2100 B/26, HE of a destroyer or torpedo boat was picked up on the port beam. The enemy is thought to have been 2 to 3 miles away. (21)

27 Sep 1941 HMS Upright (Lt. J.S. Wraith, DSC, RN) torpedoed and sank the Italian torpedo boat Albatros (334 tons, built 1934) 8 nautical miles north-west of Cape Rasocolmo near Milazzo, Sicily, Italy near position 38°24'N, 15°22'E.
The Albatros was on her way to escort the German submarine U-371 through the Straits of Messina. There were 47 survivors and 36 men missing (including two German officers).
Later a torpedo attack was carried out on an escorted merchant vessel off Cape Milazzo near position 38°20'N, 15°18'E but no hits were obtained.

The target was the tanker Lina (1235 GRT, built 1909) escorted by the small italian armed merchant cruiser D 22 / Lago Tana (783 GRT, built 1940) which reported the attack, first sighted at 1310 B/27. They had sailed from Messina at 0930 B/27 at 8.5 knots for Trapani where they arrived at 0700 B/28.
0730 B/27 - Sighted a trawler proceeding towards Messina.
0745 B/27 - Heard four distant explosions.
0820 B/27 - Sighted one torpedo boat carrying out an A/S sweep north-west of Cape Rasocolmo. Decided to attack.

0855 B/27 - Fired two torpedoes from about 3000 yards. One hit was obtained and the HE of the target ceased. The result was not seen as Upright went deep upon firing. One aircraft and two destroyers arrived in the area after half an hour and searched the area.
1020 B/27 - Three depth charges were dropped astern, not at all close.
1043 B/27 to 1105 B/27 - Three single depth charges were dropped but these were not close also.
1310 B/27 - Sighted one merchant vessel of about 2000 tons. She was escorted by a small armed merchant vessel. They were proceeding westwards along the coast near Cape Milazzo. Started attack.

1345 B/27 - Fired two torpedoes from 1800 yards. Both missed as the speed was most likely underestimated. After firing Upright went to 70 feet and altered course. Seven depth charges were dropped by the armed merchant vessel but these were not close.
2305 B/27 - While patrolling north of Cape Rasocolmo, a destroyer was sighted bearing 090° at a range of 5000 yards. Enemy course was 290° at 14 knots. HMS Upright dived to avoid detection. (21)

28 Sep 1941 At 1115 B/28, HMS Upright (Lt. J.S. Wraith, DSC, RN) sighted three minesweepers sweeping the channel near Cape Rasocolmo. HMS Upright retired to the northwards for 15 minutes to avoid them.
At 1150 B/28, the minesweepers turned round and headed back to Messina.
At 1417 B/28, two distant explosions were heard.
At 1520 B/28, a motor torpedo boat was seen proceeding westwards along the coast.
At 1710 B/28, a motor torpedo boat was seen laying stopped three miles to the eastward. (21)
29 Sep 1941 At 0045 B/29, HMS Upright (Lt. J.S. Wraith, DSC, RN) sighted a fully lit hospital ship proceeding eastwards towards Messina.

This was possibly the Italian hospital ship Virgilio (11718 GRT, built 1926) which sailed from Augusta at 1045 B/27 for Palermo and was diverted to search for survivors of the submarine chaser Albatros. Virgilio arrived at Augusta at 0920 B/29.
At 0230 B/29, a single funnel destroyer or torpedo boat was sighted at a range of 4000 yards. HMS Upright dived to avoid being sighted. Enemy course was 110° at 15 knots.

A possibility is the Italian torpedo boat Rosolino Pilo which sailed from Messina at 1930 B/28 and returned at 1320 B/29 but she was a three-funnel torpedo boat ! It could not be the Italian destroyers Maestrale, Folgore or Grecale (10th Squadriglia) which sailed on the 27th. They were escorting the Italian light cruisers Luigi di Savoia Duca degli Abruzzi and Muzio Attendolo (8th Cruiser Division) and were off Stromboli at about 0430 B/29. They arrived at Messina at 0752 B/28-0826 B/28 (unless HMS Upright’s position (DR) was way off).
At 1600 B/29, the Vichy-French hospital ship Canada (9684 GRT, built 1912) passing a mile away to the westward. She was escorted by a motor torpedo boat.
At 1650 B/29, Canada altered course to the north and her escort was seen to return towards Messina. (21)

30 Sep 1941 At 0035 B/30, HMS Upright (Lt. J.S. Wraith, DSC, RN) sighted a destroyer or torpedo boat at a range of 4000 yards. HMS Upright dived to avoid being sighted. Enemy course was 220° at 14 knots.

This was most probably either one of the Italian torpedo boats San Martino or Generale Antonino Cascino which both sailed from Messina for an A/S sweep at 1805 B/29 and returned at 0937 B/30 and 1230 B/30 respectively.
At 0740 B/30, a destroyer or torpedo boat was seen on A/S patrol. An attack was started but the enemy was zigging constantly at speed between 12 and 15 knots so the attempt to attack was abandoned.
This was most probably either one of the torpedo boats given above.
At 1050 B/30, minesweepers were sighted carrying out the usual sweep of the Cape Rasocolmo channel. Two motor torpedo boats were sighted during the forenoon but nothing else was sighted.
At 2311 B/30, a destroyer or torpedo boat was sighted at a range of 6000 yards. HMS Upright dived to avoid being sighted. Enemy course was 080° at a speed of 14 knots.
This was most probably the Italian torpedo boat Rosolino Pilo which sailed from Messina for an A/S sweep at 1856 B/30 and returned at 1755 B/1. (21)

1 Oct 1941 At 0150 B/1, north-east of Cape Calava, Sicily, HMS Upright (Lt. J.S. Wraith, DSC, RN) sighted a destroyer or torpedo boat at a range of 4000 yards. HMS Upright dived to avoid being sighted. Enemy course was 230°.
This was most probably again the torpedo boat Rosolino Pilo which sailed from Messina at 1856 B/30 for an A/S sweep and then escort U-97 and U-559. She returned to Messina at 1755 B/1.
At 0315 B/1, a motor torpedo boat was sighted at a range of 3000 yards. HMS Upright took avoiding action on the surface.

At 0640 B/1, while north of Cape Calava, Sicily, on coming to periscope depth a submarine, thought to be a U-boat of the smaller type, was sighted bearing 150°. Range was 4 miles. This submarine must have passed 2 to 3 miles to the north of HMS Upright while she was deep. The enemy was proceeding to Messina, was not zigging and her speed was estimated at 12 knots.

This was either U-97 or U-559 which made rendezvous with the Italian torpedo boat boat Rosolino Pilo not long afterwards which escorted them during their passage throught the Straits of Messina. Both U-boats were on passage to the Eastern Mediterranean having come from the Atlantic.
A torpedo attack was no longer possible and a gun action was not considered feasible under the existing conditions.
At 2300 B/1, HMS Upright left patrol to return to Malta. (21)

2 Oct 1941 At 0355 B/2, north of Cape Gallo, HMS Upright (Lt. J.S. Wraith, DSC, RN) sighted a submarine at a range of 1000 yards but the presence in the area of HMS Urge (Lt.Cdr. E.P. Tomkinson, RN) and her orders were not to attack submarines at night to avoid a case of mistaken identity. The attack was thus abandoned.
It was thought to be a large, 1000 tons, Italian submarine although she was difficult to make out against the land. Course of this submarine when sighted was 080°, speed 12 knots.
Around this time HMS Urge should have been proceeding on course 030° at only 4.5 knots as she was charging.
No German or Italian submarines are known to be in this area at this time though. (21)
3 Oct 1941 During daylight hours, HMS Upright (Lt. J.S. Wraith, DSC, RN) made the submerged passage through Q.B.B. 255 [mined zone]. (21)
4 Oct 1941 Around 0830 B/4, HMS Upright (Lt. J.S. Wraith, DSC, RN) ended her 17th war patrol (15th in the Mediterranean) at Malta. (21)

14 Oct 1941 Around 1750 A/14, HMS Upright (Lt. J.S. Wraith, DSC, RN) departed Malta for her 18th war patrol (16th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol off the south-east coast of Sicily, in position 073° - Cape Murro di Porco, 7.5 miles, to intercept three Italian destroyers of the Navigatori-class there are thought to make a high speed run to North Africa with troop and supplies.

This referred to the destroyers Emanuele Pessagno (310 troops), Antonio Da Noli (295 troops) and Nicolò Zeno (268 troops) which sailed from Augusta at 1830 B/15 - 1850 B/15. They were transporting German troops to Benghazi where they arrived at 1630 B/16 - 2030 B/16.

15 Oct 1941 At 0905 A/15, in position 36°49'N, 15°35'E, HMS Upright (Lt. J.S. Wraith, DSC, RN) was passed at a range of 1500 yards by the Italian hospital ship Virgilio (11718 GRT, built 1928). Enemy course was 150° at 15 knots.
Virgilio had sailed at 0730 B/15 from Augusta bound for Tripoli.
At 2200 A/15, HMS Upright left patrol and set course to return to Malta. The three Italian troop transporting destroyers had not been sighted. (21)
16 Oct 1941 Around 1230 A/16, HMS Upright (Lt. J.S. Wraith, DSC, RN) ended her 18th war patrol (16th in the Mediterranean) at Malta. (21)

19 Oct 1941 Around 1630 A/19, HMS Upright (Lt. J.S. Wraith, DSC, RN) departed Malta for her 19th war patrol (17th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol off the North-West coast of Sicily between Marettimo Island and Cape San Vito, not to the north of 38°40'N, and between 12°00'E and 12°35'E.
20 Oct 1941 During daylight hours, HMS Upright (Lt. J.S. Wraith, DSC, RN) made the submerged passage of Q.B.B. 65 [mined area]. (21)
21 Oct 1941 At 0230 B/21, HMS Upright (Lt. J.S. Wraith, DSC, RN) entered her patrol area.

The Capt. S 10's in a signal timed 1645 A/20, reported a northbound enemy convoy of a transport, a tanker and three destroyers as escort. HMS Upright was to patrol in position 38°15'N, 12°10'E. It was expected the convoy would pass through this position around 0130 A/22.

This referred to the convoy made up of the tanker Proserpina (Italian, former French Beauce, 4869 GRT, built 1926) and the transport Giulia (Italian, 5921 GRT, built 1925) escorted by the destroyers Nicoloso Da Recco, Folgore and Antoniotto Usodimare. They had departed from Tripoli at 1555 B/20-1730 B/20 for Naples but were ordered back when it was learned that an enemy force of two cruisers and two destroyers was at sea. They returned to Tripoli at 1320 B/21-1325 B/21.

The enemy force referred to by the Italians was ' Force K ', made up of the light cruisers HMS Aurora (Capt. W.G. Agnew, RN), HMS Penelope (Capt. A.D. Nicholl, RN) and the destroyers HMS Lance (Lt.Cdr. R.W.F. Northcott, RN) and HMS Lively (Lt.Cdr. W.F.E. Hussey, DSC, RN) which were on passage from Gibraltar to Malta.

At 1900 A/21, HMS Upright surfaced and set course to intercept a convoy of two merchant ships reported to her in the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1054 A/21. [We have not been able to find any more info on this signal.]

This probably referred again to the Proserpina-convoy (see DEFE3/690/frame 119, 128). ULTRA had also intercepted a signal referring to the Italian transport Nirvo (5164 GRT, built 1919) escorted by the Italian destroyer Alfredo Oriani (see DEFE3/690/frame 121) which was expected to sail from Tripoli at 1300 B/20 and pass through a point 10 miles south of Marettimo at 0330 B/22. However, another ULTRA intercept (DEFE3/690/frame 141) indicated that Nirvo had returned to port at midday 21st.
At 2200 A/21, HMS Upright arrived in the ordered patrol position and commenced to patrol on north and south courses but nothing was sighted all night. (32)

23 Oct 1941 At 0930 A/23, while at periscope depth to cross the 100 fathom line in position 38°07'6"N, 12°14'8"E, HMS Upright (Lt. J.S. Wraith, DSC, RN) felt two heavy bumps and a loud scraping noise was heard down the ships side. Nothing could be seen through the periscope. It was thought this might have been a mine, so course was altered to the northward. HMS Upright kept outside the 100 fathom line in this vicinity for the remainder of the patrol. (21)

23 Oct 1941 In a signal timed 1929 A/23, the Capt. S.10 informed HMS Upright (Lt. J.S. Wraith, DSC, RN) and HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) of the following; Aircraft reconnaissance reported at 0700 A/23 a 3000-ton tanker close off Kelibia course north.

This 'tanker' may have been the Italian transport Perseo (5225 GRT, built 1921) which sailed from Sousse at 1700 B/22 at 5 knots and arrived at Cagliari at 2000 B/24. [Perseo was an engine aft ship which looked thus like a tanker.] (33)
29 Oct 1941 At 0001 A/29, HMS Upright (Lt. J.S. Wraith, DSC, RN) left patrol to return to Malta as had been ordered in the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1047 A/26.
During daylight hours she made the submerged passage of Q.B.B. 65 [mined area] at 120 feet. (21)
30 Oct 1941 Around 0700 A/30, HMS Upright (Lt. J.S. Wraith, DSC, RN) ended her 19th war patrol (17th in the Mediterranean) at Malta. The patrol had been uneventful. (21)

5 Nov 1941 HMS Upright (Lt. J.S. Wraith, DSC, RN) conducted attack exercises off Malta during which the destroyers HMS Lance (Lt.Cdr. R.W.F. Northcott, RN) and HMS Lively (Lt.Cdr. W.F.E. Hussey, DSC, RN) acted as targets.

11 Nov 1941 Around 2000 B/11, HMS Upright (Lt. J.S. Wraith, DSC, RN) departed Malta for her 20th war patrol (18th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol in the Ionian Sea in position 37°50'N, 19°27'E. She was to form a patrol line with HMS Urge (Lt.Cdr. E.P. Tomkinson, RN) which was to patrol 12 miles to the south-east of HMS Upright.
On the way to her patrol area HMS Upright was to proceed through position 35°51'N, 15°15'E at 0001 B/12.

12 Nov 1941 In a signal timed 1915 Z/12, the Capt. S.10 informed HMS Upright (Lt. J.S. Wraith, DSC, RN) of the fact that at 1550 B/12, three enemy cruisers had been sighted in position 39°23'N, 17°22'E proceeding on course 170° at 20 knots.
This signal was received by HMS Upright at 1950 B/12.
These cruisers would cross Upright's line of advance 35 miles ahead around 2230 B/12 if their course and speed were constant.
This signal referred to the destroyers Aviere, Camicia Nere and Geniere which had sailed from Taranto at 1130 B/12-1205 B/12 and arrived at Messina at 0110 B/13-0310 B/13.
At 2000 B/12, HMS Upright increased speed to 10 knots which was the maximum in the rough sea conditions.
By 0100 B/13, nothing had been sighted and HMS Upright slowed down to 4 knots on one engine, charging the batteries with the other. (35)

13 Nov 1941 In a signal timed 2355 B/13, the Capt. S.10 informed HMS Upright (Lt. J.S. Wraith, DSC, RN) that two northbound supply ships escorted by one destroyer had been reported [we have been unable to find the exact text of this signal].

This signal most probably referred to the Italian transports Capo Arma (3172 GRT, built 1905) and Capo Faro (3476 GRT, built 1905) escorted by the Italian torpedo boat Pegaso. ULTRA had revealed that they had sailed from Benghazi probably at 1600 B/12 through position 37°05'N, 20°40'E at 0430 Z/14 (note Z=GMT time). They actually sailed at 1730 B/12 in heavy weather and arrived at Brindisi at 1300 B/15. (21)

14 Nov 1941 At 1230 B/14, on surfacing for a sun sight, HMS Upright (Lt. J.S. Wraith, DSC, RN) sighted the masts and funnels of two (small) merchant ships on bearing 300° at a range of 8 miles. No escort could be seen. Enemy course was 340°.
It was not possible to close the enemy on the surface as the sea conditions greatly reduced the speed of the submarine. The sea state also made it impossible to man the deck gun.
This were probably the Capo Arma and Capo Faro escorted by Pegaso (see the event for 13 November for more details on this convoy).
At 2325 B/14, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1006 B/14 was received [see the event below]. Course was set to comply. (21)

14 Nov 1941 In a signal timed 1006 B/14, Capt. S.10 ordered HMS Upright (Lt. J.S. Wraith, DSC, RN), HMS Urge (Lt.Cdr. E.P. Tomkinson, RN) and HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) to shift the patrol line 053 - 27 miles to be in position to intercept two fast southbound supply ships.

This must have referred to the convoy made up of the troop transports (naval auxiliaries) Città di Genova (Italian, 5413 GRT, built 1930) and Città di Napoli (5418 GRT, built 1929) which were to proceed from Taranto to Benghasi escorted by the destroyers Antonio Pigafetta and Giovanni da Verrazzano.
The position of HMS Urge to intercept this convoy was the result of an ULTRA intercept. [Code breaking of the enemy's naval cypher, source: file DEFE3/690/462, (National Archives, London)]. (36)
15 Nov 1941 At 0600 B/15, HMS Upright (Lt. J.S. Wraith, DSC, RN) dived in her new patrol position.
At 1125 B/15, a distant explosion was heard which was followed by two more faint ones.
At 1930 B/15, HMS Upright surfaced and set course to return to her original patrol position in accordance with the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1055 B/14. (21)

16 Nov 1941 In a signal timed 1731 B/16 the Capt. S.1 sent the following orders; Important southbound convoy sighted off Brindisi at 1600 B/16 probably passing westward of Cephalonia and Zante (Zakinthos) daylight hours 17th. Establish a patrol line forthwith. 4 - 38°01'N, 20°13'E - 280° - 12 - 190° - 30. Positions to be taken up as follows: ORP Sokol (Kpt.mar. (Lt.Cdr.) B. Karnicki) in position 'A', 38°01'N, 20°13'E, HMS Urge (Lt.Cdr. E.P. Tomkinson, RN) in position 'B', HMS Upright (Lt. J.S. Wraith, DSC, RN) in position 'C' and HMS Thunderbolt (Lt. C.B. Crouch, DSO, RN) in position 'D'. Until further orders submarines are not to be attacked. Submarines in conjunction endeavour to establish SS/T communication when safe to do so. (36)
17 Nov 1941 HMS Upright (Lt. J.S. Wraith, DSC, RN) made a torpedo attack on a convoy about 50 nautical miles west of Cephalonia Island, Greece. No hits were obtained.

This convoy, was the one that was announced in the Capt. S.1's signal timed 1731 B/16 and was made up of the German transport Tinos (2826 GRT, built 1914) and the Italian transport Bolsena (2384 GRT, built 1918) escorted by the Italian torpedo boat Orione.

They had sailed from Brindisi at 1530 B/16. At 1450 B/17, they were attacked by six Blenheim bombers in position 37°49'N, 19°45'E and arrived at Navarino at 0740 B/18. Apparently, the attack by the submarine was unobserved.
1110 B/17 - Sighted an aircraft and smoke to the north. Closed.
1125 B/17 - Went to periscope depth and sighted masts and funnels of two merchant ships and one destroyer. It appeared they were passing out of range. Went deep again.
1140 B/17 - Returned to periscope depth and noticed that the enemy had turned towards. Started attack.
1150 B/17 - The convoy altered course back to the original course. The attack now had to be carried out at a greater range than was hoped for. The merchant ships were of about 3000 tons.
1205 B/17 - In position 38°09'N, 19°29'E fired four torpedoes, two at each merchant ship from about 5000 yards. No hits were obtained.
1305 B/17 - Five explosions were heard. [This was HMS Urge (Lt.Cdr. E.P. Tomkinson, RN) attacking and being hunted.]
1330 B/17 - Surfaced to pass an enemy report.
1332 B/17 - Dived for an aircraft. Commenced to reload the torpedo tubes.
1500 B/17 - Surfaced again to try to pass an enemy report. Could not get through to Malta. The attempt was finally successful at 1535 B/17 and the enemy report was broadcasted twice.
1540 B/17 - Dived on completion of passing the enemy report. (37)

18 Nov 1941 In a signal timed 1641 A/18, the Capt. S.1 cancelled the orders as had been given in his signal timed 1731 B/16. New orders were as follows; ORP Sokol (Kpt.mar. (Lt.Cdr.) B. Karnicki) proceeding to Navarino as had already been ordered in a signal timed 1113 B/18. HMS Urge (Lt.Cdr. E.P. Tomkinson, RN) returning Malta forthwith through positions 38°13'N, 20°00'E, 38°18'N, 19°21'E and 35°51'N, 15°15'E. HMS Upright (Lt. J.S. Wraith, DSC, RN) was to proceed forthwith to position 37°37'N, 19°38'E to a new patrol position that will be signalled in a later signal. HMS Thunderbolt (Lt. C.B. Crouch, DSO, RN) was to remain north of 37°50'N until 0200 B/19 and then proceed through positions 37°29'N, 20°00'E and 36°27'N, 22°00'E to establish patrol in the southern Aegean. Submarines are not to be attacked during the night of the 18th/l9th. (36)

18 Nov 1941 In a signal timed 1647 B/18, the Capt. S.1 sent the following orders to the submarines listed below; Establish a patrol line as quickly as possible; 4 - 36°35'N, 18°30'E - 200° - 15 - 290° - 40. Positions are to be taken up as follows; HMS Upright (Lt. J.S. Wraith, DSC, RN) in position (A) 36°35'N, 18°30'E. HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.deB. Kershaw, RN) in position (B). HMS Trusty (Lt.Cdr. W.D.A. King, DSO, DSC, RN) in position (C). HMS Perseus (Lt.Cdr. E.C.F. Nicolay, DSO, RN) in position (D). Route to position HMS Upright as laid down in my signal timed 1641 B/18 remainder as routed by Capt. S.10. After arrival in vicinity patrol position submarines are not to be attacked. Adjacent submarines are to endeavour to communicate on SS/T.

In a follow up signal timed 2155 B/18, the Capt. S.1 cancelled his orders given to HMS Thunderbolt (Lt. C.B. Crouch, DSO, RN) in his signal timed 1641 B/18. In the signal timed 1847 B/18 for HMS Perseus, HMS Thunderbolt should be read throughout. She was to remaining to northward of 37°50'N, until 0200 B/19 then proceedeed through position 37°29'N, 20°00'E direct to position (D) 35°53'N, 18°12'E. (36)
19 Nov 1941 At 0040 B/19, HMS Upright (Lt. J.S. Wraith, DSC, RN) received the Capt. S.1's signal timed 1647 B/18.
At 1030 B/19, HMS Upright dived in her new patrol position after having made a two hour run on the surface at 11 knots towards it. (21)

21 Nov 1941 In a signal timed 1611 B/21, the Capt. S.1, referring to his signal timed 1647 B/18, ordered the four submarines in the patrol line, HMS Upright (Lt. J.S. Wraith, DSC, RN), HMS P 31 (Lt. J.B.deB. Kershaw, RN), HMS Trusty (Lt.Cdr. W.D.A. King, DSO, DSC, RN) and HMS Thunderbolt (Lt. C.B. Crouch, DSO, RN), to move their patrol positions 082° - 45 miles. (36)
22 Nov 1941 At 0610 B/22, HMS Upright (Lt. J.S. Wraith, DSC, RN) dived in her new patrol position.
At 1325 B/22, in position 36°39'N, 19°27'E, the Italian cruiser Luigi Cadorna was sighted. Enemy course was 100° at 18 knots. She was not escorted and passed 7 miles to the south of HMS Upright.

The Italian cruiser was carrying stores and troops which sailed from Brindisi at 1850 B/21, escorted by the Italian destroyer Augusto Riboty until 0000 B/22, when the latter turned back and arrived at Brindisi at 0736 B/22. The cruiser arrived at Benghazi at 1125 B/23.
At 1450 B/22, HMS Upright surfaced to pass an enemy report which was successful at 1532 B/22. (21)

23 Nov 1941 At 0027 B/23, HMS Upright (Lt. J.S. Wraith, DSC, RN) received the Capt. S.1's signal timed 2021 B/22 in which she was ordered to leave patrol and proceed to Malta passing through position 37°06'N, 17°40'E. Further orders would be signalled by the Capt. S.10. Course was altered to comply with these orders.

At 2112 B/23, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1129 B/23 was received which stated the following; Referring to the Capt. S.1's signal timed 2021 B/22, proceed through position 35°51'N, 15°15'E to arrive Malta before 1800 B/24 or at 0730 B/25. (35)
25 Nov 1941 At 0730 B/25, HMS Upright (Lt. J.S. Wraith, DSC, RN) ended her 20th war patrol (18th in the Mediterranean) at Malta.
In the afternoon she was docked in No.1 graving dock at the Malta Dockyard. (37)
28 Nov 1941 HMS Upright (Lt. J.S. Wraith, DSC, RN) is undocked. (38)

4 Dec 1941 Around 1700 B/4, HMS Upright (Lt. J.S. Wraith, DSC, RN) departed Malta for her 21st war patrol (19th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol to the south of the Straits of Messina (area 'E' concentrating in the vicinity of position 37°57'N, 15°30'E).

5 Dec 1941 Between 1338 B/5 and 1447 B/5, HMS Upright (Lt. J.S. Wraith, DSC, RN) heard at least 30 depth charges exploding. It was estimated that these were about 10 to 15 miles away. It was thought the depth charges may be directed against HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN) which was indeed a correct assumption as this submarine was indeed hunted and depth charged following an attack on an enemy convoy. (37)
6 Dec 1941 At 0301 B/6, well east of Catania, Sicily, HMS Upright (Lt. J.S. Wraith, DSC, RN) sighted a red flare being dropped bearing 240° 3 to 5 miles away.
At 0307 B/6, an object was sighted ahead, which was subsequently identified as a motor torpedo boat.
At 0315 B/6, HMS Upright dived to avoid detection. The motor torpedo boat passed 4 to 5 miles ahead from starboard to port. Visibility was 7 to 8 miles due to bright moonlight.
At 0405 B/6, HMS Upright surfaced and continued her passage to the patrol position.

At 0505 B/6, another red flare was dropped 3 miles ahead. HMS Upright dived. She was now 15 miles short of her patrol position but remained dived as it was considered that enemy aircraft were carrying out an intensive search of the area in conjunction with service craft.

At 1330 B/6, south of Cape dell'Armi, in approximate position 37°50'N, 15°45'E, a submarine chaser was sighted bearing 340° at a range of 5 miles. This chaser remained stopped for half an hour and then went away to the north.

Following, the attack by HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN), the Italian motor torpedo boats MAS 547 and MAS 562 sailed from Messina at 1430 B/5 for an A/S sweep and returned at 2005 B/5. But this was on the previous day. They sailed again for another A/S sweep at 0700 B/6 and returned at 1820 B/6. Perhaps one of them was the submarine chaser sighted at 1330 B/6.
At 1500 B/6, HMS Upright altered course 180° to clear the land before surfacing for the night.

At 1910 B/6, HMS Upright surfaced. Red flares were dropped off Augusta and Catania at frequent intervals during the night, presumably from aircraft coming in to land. Many lights were visible on the Sicilian coast.

There were the following movements in the area, but none appear to have been spotted by Upright: At 0930 B/6, the Italian torpedo boat Giuseppe Sirtori arrived at Messina from Taranto after escorting the Italian transports Vertunno (3159 GRT, built 1905) and Tigrai (1302 GRT, built 1918) and hunting HMS P 34 which had attacked them on the previous day.

The Italian destroyers Ascari and Carabiniere sailed from Messina at 1200 B/6 and arrived at Reggio (Calabria) where they embarked jerry cans of fuel. They sailed at midnight for Benghazi but were ordered to Navarino when it was learned that Benghazi was under a naval bombardment. Their destination was later changed to Suda, where they arrived at 1557 B/8 and landed their cargo of fuel. (37)

6 Dec 1941 In a signal timed 2121 B/6, the Capt. S.10 sent the following signal to HMS Unique (Lt. A.F. Collett, DSC, RN), HMS Upright (Lt. J.S. Wraith, DSC, RN) and HMS P 34 (Lt. P.R.H. Harrison, DSC, RN); Exceptional vigilance is necessary against U-boats, majority southbound passage of Straits probably at night. (39)
7 Dec 1941 At 0230 B/7, HMS Upright (Lt. J.S. Wraith, DSC, RN) sighted two motor torpedo boats at a range of 5 miles. They were coming towards so HMS Upright dived to avoid detection.

At 0330 B/7, HE of the motor torpedo boats could still be heard. Came to periscope depth. Visibility was 2 - 3 miles. It was decided to remain dived and proceed back to the patrol position (37°57'N, 15°30'E). The sea was very calm and there was bright moonlight making it a risky procedure to patrol in the above position on the surface. A/S conditions were exceptionally good.

We have been unable to identify these motor torpedo boats. Only the following could be found in the Italian documents available to us. The Italian motor torpedo boats MAS 556 and MAS 562 sailed from Messina at 0650 B/7 for an A/S sweep and returned at 1615 B/7 but the time does not match.
At 1105 B/7, two faint explosions were heard.
At 1200 B/7, at periscope depth it was noticed that the sea was glassy. Went to 70 feet.
At 1400 B/7, at periscope depth it was noticed that there was now a slight ripple on the surface.
At 1517 B/7, three distant explosions were heard.
At 1540 B/7, a Generali-class torpedo boat was seen carrying out an A/S sweep. Range was 7 miles. HMS Upright went to 70 feet.
At 1610 B/7, the torpedo boat passed down the starboard side at 13 knots.
This was the Italian torpedo boat Giuseppe Sirtori carrying an A/S sweep. She had sailed from Messina at 0600 B/7 and returned at 2345 B/7.
At 1630 B/7, HMS Upright returned to periscope depth. There was nothing in sight.
At 1730 B/7, a destroyer or torpedo boat was seen bearing 070° at a range of 10 miles. The enemy was steering away.
This was probably again the Giuseppe Sirtori.
At 1910 B/7, HMS Upright surfaced. It was a dark night. The moon had not risen.
At 1915 B/7, HE was heard and two minutes later a destroyer or torpedo boat was seen at 4000 yards and closing. Dived to evade.
Yet again this was most probably the Giuseppe Sirtori.
At 1922 B/7, the enemy stopped her engines. She may have heard HMS Upright diving.
At 1930 B/7, the enemy restarted her engines and made off towards Messina.
At 2000 B/7, HMS Upright surfaced. There was nothing in sight. (37)

8 Dec 1941 At 0330 B/8, HMS Upright (Lt. J.S. Wraith, DSC, RN) dived and steered towards the patrol position (37°57'N, 15°30'E). The sea was calm and there was bright moonlight. Periscope visibility was 2 - 3 miles. A/S conditions were very good.
At 1310 B/8, a submarine chaser was sighted 7 miles away. She was laying stopped, obviously listening. She started up and altered course away 20 minutes later.
At 1910 B/8, HMS Upright surfaced.

At 2225 B/8, two destroyers or torpedo boats were sighted at a range of 2000 to 3000 yards. One minute later HMS Unbeaten dived and the destroyers passed overhead at 2233 B/8 steering towards Messina.
At 2245 B/8, HMS Unbeaten surfaced. There was nothing in sight.
The following movements movements in the Messina area could be found in the available documentation, but nothing seems to match:
The Italian torpedo boat Giuseppe Sirtori sailed from Messina at 0620 B/8 for an A/S sweep and returned at 1415 B/8.
The Italian torpedo boat Generale Carlo Montanari sailed from Messina at 0830 B/8 escorting a convoy (Etruria and Delfin) to Taranto where they arrived at 1735 B/9.

The Italian torpedo boat Giuseppe Dezza sailed from Messina at 1405 B/8 to escort a U-boat. This must have been U-205 and returned at 2140 B/8. The U-boat however proceeded northwards towards La Spezia.
At 1810 B/8, the Italian motor torpedo boats MAS 556 and MAS 547 sailed from Messina for an A/S sweep and returned at 1915 B/8. (37)
9 Dec 1941 At 0545 B/9, HMS Upright (Lt. J.S. Wraith, DSC, RN) dived.
At 0630 B/9, HE from a destroyer or torpedo boat proceeding northwards was picked up. Revs. accounted for a speed of 16 knots. It was too dark to attempt an attack.

The HE picked up may have possibly been from (either one of) the Italian destroyers Granatiere, Fuciliere, Bersagliere or Alpino which sailed from Taranto at 1600 B/8-1609 B/8 and arrived at Naples at 1805 B/9-1900 B/9.

At 0809 B/9, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 0413 B/9 was received ordering HMS Upright to establish patrol in the Gulf of Taranto in position 39°30'N, 17°30'E. Course was set to comply, passing well clear of the patrol position of HMS Unique (Lt. A.F. Collett, DSC, RN).
At 1445 B/9, a series of explosions were heard.
At 1905 B/9, HMS Upright surfaced and continued her passage to her new patrol position. is ordered to a new patrol position in the Gulf of Taranto. (37)

9 Dec 1941 In a signal timed 1928 B/9, HMS Unbeaten (Lt. Cdr. E.A. Woodward, RN), HMS Upright (Lt. J.S. Wraith, DSC, RN), HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. R.D. Cayley, DSO, RN) were ordered that no northbound merchant ships of more than 2000 tons and less than 10000 tons whether escorted or not is to be attacked by you from receipt of this message until noon 11/l2 unless it is definitely identified as a tanker.
The above signal was cancelled in Capt. S.10's signal timed 0104 B/10. (39)

10 Dec 1941 At 0122 B/10, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1654 B/9 was received in which HMS Upright's (Lt. J.S. Wraith, DSC, RN) patrol position was adjusted to 38°27'N, 17°24'E (which was to the south-south-east of Cape Rizzuto and no longer in the Gulf of Taranto). (40)

11 Dec 1941 At 0540 B/11, HMS Upright (Lt. J.S. Wraith, DSC, RN) sighted a destroyer or torpedo boat right ahead coming towards. Five minutes later HMS Upright dived when the range to the destroyer was 3000 yards.
We have been unable to identify this vessel.
At 0740 B/11, three faint explosions were heard.
At 0800 B/11, HMS Upright arrived in her new patrol position (38°27'N, 17°24'E). Two schooners were seen 4 miles away to the west.

At 0900 B/11, one of the schooners proceeded to the west. The other remained exactly in HMS Upright's patrol position for the next 24 hours. She is thought to have been an A/S schooner. She was not attacked with gunfire to not compromise the patrol position.

At 1553 B/11, two destroyers were sighted at a range of 7 miles. They were proceeding northwards. An attack was commenced and the range closed to 3000 yards but two large zigs away made the success of the attack very doubtful so it was broken off. The destroyers appeared to be one of the Navigatori-class and one of the Maestrale-class. They were doing 20 knots and disappeared on a mean course of 340° towards Taranto.
This were most probably the destroyers Lanzerotto Malocello and Saetta which sailed from Trapani at 1645 B/10-1737 B/10 and arrived at Taranto at 2146 B/11-0100 B/12.
At 1707 B/11, the Capt. S.1's signal timed 1311 B/11 was received which reported the two destroyers encountered at 1553 B/11. This confirmed their speed of 20 knots.
At 1710 B/11, 10 faint explosions were heard.
At 1905 B/11, HMS Upright surfaced and retired 8 miles to the south to charge, well clear of the enemy A/S schooner. (37)
11 Dec 1941 HMS Upright (Lt. J.S. Wraith, DSC, RN) patrolled on course 090° - 270° during the night.

In a signal timed 1959 B/11, the Capt. S.10 ordered HMS Upright to leave leave patrol 0001 B/14 to arrive at Malta at 0730 B/16 proceeding through positions 39°05'N, 17°30'E, 37°46'N, 16°06'E and 35°51'N, 15°15'E on the way back. (40)

11 Dec 1941 In a signal timed 0924 B/11, Capt. S.10 ordered HMS Unbeaten (Lt. Cdr. E.A. Woodward, RN), HMS Upright (Lt. J.S. Wraith, DSC, RN), HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. R.D. Cayley, DSO, RN) to form R line as follows: 3 - 39°27'N, 17°24'E - 060° - 13 - 150° - 20. Position A (39°27'N, 17°24'E), HMS Upright, Position B, HMS Utmost and position C, HMS Unbeaten. (39)

12 Dec 1941 At 1000 B/12, while dived, HMS Upright (Lt. J.S. Wraith, DSC, RN) sighted the schooner, first seen the previous day, still laying stopped near the patrol position. But she now got underway and left the area to the west.
At 1910 B/12, HMS Upright surfaced and proceeded to her new patrol position (in the Gulf of Taranto in 40°08'N, 17°00'E) as had been ordered in the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1034 B/12. (40)

12 Dec 1941 In a signal timed 1034 B/12, Capt. S.10 gave the following orders to HMS Unbeaten (Lt. Cdr. E.A. Woodward, RN), HMS Upright (Lt. J.S. Wraith, DSC, RN), HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. R.D. Cayley, DSO, RN);

Proceed forthwith to establish patrol during daylight hours on 13 December as follows: Upright in position 40°08'N, 17°00'E, Utmost in position 40°00'N, 17°11'E, Unbeaten in position 40°12'N, 17°19'E. Attention is called to QB 4 (an Italian minefield) Heavy units may leave Taranto A.M./13 proceeding south. After attack or at 1930 B/13 withdraw to original patrol as in my signal 0924 B/11.

In a signal timed 1615 B/12, the Capt. S.10 infored the above submarines that two merchant vessels and three destroyers had been spotted at 1505 B/12 in position 38°00'N, 17°00'E proceeding on course 030° at 10 knots. (39)

13 Dec 1941 HMS Upright (Lt. J.S. Wraith, DSC, RN) torpedoed and sank the Italian transports Fabio Filzi (6836 GRT, built 1940) and Carlo del Greco (6837 GRT, built 1941) in the Gulf of Taranto in approximate position 40°08'N, 17°00'E.

Considering the relative long range of this attack and that the targets were doing 15 knots, this was a most remarkable attack relying heavily on ASDIC with at least three torpedo hits (two on Fabio Filzi and one (perhaps two) on Carlo del Greco). According to Italian sources there were 432 survivors, 217 men were killed or missing. This convoy, from Messina to Taranto, was escorted by the Italian destroyers Nicoloso da Recco and Antoniotto Usodimare. The Italian torpedo boat Giuseppe Dezza was initially also part of the convoy escort by was no longer present when HMS Upright made her attack having parted company earlier near Crotone to return to Messina.
0150 B/13 - In approximate position 40°08'N, 17°00'E HE was heard approaching from the South. Turned towards.
0155 B/13 - HE became louder and was passing down the Port side. Changed course to obtain a favourable attack position.
0203 B/13 - Sighted a large ship followed by a smaller one. Shortly afterwards sighted another large ship following the other ones. Started attack.

0212 B/13 - Fired four torpedoes at the two big ships when they overlapped. Shortly after firing Upright dived. All four torpedoes hit the targets. It was thought the first three torpedoes hit the first target and the last torpedo hit the second target. Depth charging started.
0228 B/13 - A ship was heard breaking up.
0341 B/13 - A second ship was heard breaking up.
0737 B/13 - The last depth charge was dropped. In all 48 had been dropped.

0800 B/13 - Came to periscope depth. Black smoke was hanging about near the scene of the attack. No ships were sighted. Decided not to return to the patrol position which was no compromised, but to patrol to the southwest of HMS Utmost [which patrol position was 40°00'N, 17°11'E], keeping well clear of her.
0906 B/13 - Heard a loud explosion, like a torpedo hitting [No torpedo attacks by Allied submarines were made at this time in this area].
Between 0937 B/13 and 0957 B/13, 11 depth charge explosions were heard.
1645 B/13 - Heard 5 distant depth charge explosions.
1905 B/13 - Surfaced and set course to the original patrol position, 40°08'N, 17°00'E.

2326 B/13, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 2037 B/13 was received. HMS Upright was to remain in the patrol position as had been ordered in the timed 1034 B/12 until 1400 B/14. As the attack has compromised this position it was decided to remain in the present position. (37)

13 Dec 1941 In a signal timed 2037 B/13, Capt. S.10 ordered HMS Unbeaten (Lt. Cdr. E.A. Woodward, RN), HMS Upright (Lt. J.S. Wraith, DSC, RN) and HMS Utmost (Lt.Cdr. R.D. Cayley, DSO, RN) to remain in position ordered as in his signal timed 1034 B/12, until attack or 1000 B/14 then returning to the original patrol line as ordered in his signal timed 0924 B/11. Big game was to be expected. (39)
14 Dec 1941 At 0215 B/14, HMS Upright (Lt. J.S. Wraith, DSC, RN), which had retired 12 miles to the south-east for this purpose, sent an enemy and attack report to the Capt. S.10.
At 0330 B/14, HMS Upright was back in her patrol position.
At 0358 B/14, HE was picked up to the north.
At 0400 B/14, a destroyer was sighted coming from the north at high speed. It was steering straight towards so HMS Utmost dived.

The destroyer must have seen the submarine dive as she commenced hunting and dropping depth charges fairly close just before passing overhead four minutes later. The first 5 depth charges out of a total of 15 were close.

This was the Italian destroyer Maestrale. She had sailed at 1435 B/13 from Taranto with the Caio Duilio squadron and been detached with Corazziere, Vincenzo Gioberti and Alfredo Oriani to reinforce the escort of the Vittorio Veneto squadron.
It is thought HMS Upright had been fixed by D/F bearings while transferring her reports.
At 1647 B/14, two large destroyers passed 6 miles to the east at 20 knots on a course of 160°. (37)

15 Dec 1941 In a signal timed 1150 B/15, the Capt. S.10 ordered HMS Upright (Lt. J.S. Wraith, DSC, RN) to leave patrol proceeding through position 39°10'N, 17°30'E at 1900 B/15, thence through 37°30'N, 16°45'E and 35°51'N, 15°15'E to arrive at alta 0730 B/18.
This signal was received at 1310 B/15.
At 1345 B/15, an A/S schooner was sighted laying stopped in the same position as on 11 and 12 December.
At 1900 B/15, HMS Upright surfaced and set course for Malta. (40)
18 Dec 1941 At 0730 B/18, HMS Upright (Lt. J.S. Wraith, DSC, RN) ended her 21st war patrol (19th in the Mediterranean) at Malta. (37)

1 Jan 1942 Around 1830 B/1, HMS Upright (Lt. J.S. Wraith, DSC, RN) departed Malta for her 22nd war patrol (20th in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol between Lampedusa and the Kerkennah shallows in area 'L' (34°24'N, 11°47'E, 345° - 30 - 60). Liberty of action was also granted provisionally to patrol south of 34°50'N, and west of 12°40'E.

2 Jan 1942 In a signal timed 1141 B/2, the Vice-Admiral Malta, informed HMS Upright (Lt. J.S. Wraith, DSC, RN) of the following; One merchant ship one destroyer, course 225° at 5 knots, position 34°42'N, 11°27'E at 1045 B/2.
This most probably referred to the transport Sturla (German, 1397 GRT, built 1933) escorted by the Italian torpedo boat Calliope.

Calliope had sailed from Tripoli at 1800 B/1 to rendezvous with the German transport Sturla at 0800 B/2 off Kerkennah buoy no. 4. The German ship was disguised in French colours and it took a bit of time before the confusion was cleared. They arrived at Tripoli at 0940 B/3. (42)

4 Jan 1942 In a signal timed 1247 B/4, the Capt. S.10, ordered HMS Upright (Lt. J.S. Wraith, DSC, RN) to proceed with despatch to a new patrol position, about 50 nautical miles east of Tripoli, Libya (32°56'N, 14°25'E). The signal was received at 1400 B/4 and course was set to this position keeping 10 miles north of the minefield to the north of Tripoli. (43)

5 Jan 1942 In a signal timed 1007 B/5, the Capt. S.10 ordered HMS Upright (Lt. J.S. Wraith, DSC, RN) to resume patrol in area 'L'. The signal was only received at 2357 B/5 and course was immediately altered to return to that area. (41)

5 Jan 1942 In a signal timed 1057 B/5, the Vice-Admiral Malta informed HMS Upright (Lt. J.S. Wraith, DSC, RN) and HMS Urge (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN) that two enemy destroyers had been sighted at 1015 B/5 to the north-east of Khoms, Libya in position 32°49'N, 14°41'E proceeding on course 205° at 15 knots.

In a signal timed 1213 B/5, the Vice-Admiral Malta informed HMS Upright and HMS Urge then an enemy battleship with three cruisers and four destroyers had been reported that morning to the north-east of Khoms, Libya in position 32°59'N, 14°35'E proceeding on course 70° at 15 knots. This most likely referred to the close cover force for a convoy. This close cover force was made up of the battleship Caio Duilio, light cruisers Giuseppe Garibaldi, Emanuele Filiberto Duca d'Aosta, Raimondo Montecuccoli, Muzio Attendolo and the destroyers Maestrale, Vincenzo Gioberti, Alfredo Oriani, Scirocco and Lanzerotto Malocello.

In a signal timed 1945 B/5, the Capt. S.10 informed HMS Upright and HMS Urge of the following; Small 9 knots and medium 7 knots merchant ship(s) close west of Tripoli at 1800 B/5. Anticipate they are using route past Turgeuness and arriving Kerkenah about noon 6th. All merchant ships encountered are to be attacked. (42)

6 Jan 1942 In a signal timed 1523 B/6, the Capt. S.10 informed HMS Upright (Lt. J.S. Wraith, DSC, RN) and HMS Urge (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN) that a large merchant ship and one destroyer had been sighted at 1000 B/6, in position 36°09'N, 10°40'E proceeding on course 109° at 10 knots. Aircraft will endeavour to attack north of Kerkennah after dark tonight.

This was the Italian transport Delia (5406 GRT, built 1917) escorted by the torpedo boat Cigno. She arrived in Sousse at 1100 B/6 due to the bad weather. She had sailed at 2345 B/4-2400 B/4 from Palermo for Tripoli, escorted by the torpedo boat Cigno. The torpedo boat was supposed to leave her at dawn on the 6th but escorted her as far as Sousse. At 1020 B/6, when 10 miles from Sousse, Cigno observed a Blenheim bomber (most certainly the one which reported them). At 1800 B/6, the torpedo boat Calliope sailed from Tripoli to escort Delia from Kerkenah buoy no.5. They joined there at 0800 B/7. At 1125 B/7, they were attacked by a single Wellington bomber which was shot down by Calliope. They arrived at Tripoli at 0840 B/8. (42)

6 Jan 1942 In a signal timed 2357 B/6, the Capt. S.10 informed HMS Upright (Lt. J.S. Wraith, DSC, RN) and HMS Urge (Lt. J.D. Martin, RN) that at 2300 B/6 an aircraft had reported two medium merchant vessels and two destroyers in position 35°47'N. 11°23'E proceeding on course 360° at 8 knots. They were also informed that aircraft were at that moment taking off from Malta to attack the reported enemy ships.

This must have referred to the Italian transport Perla (5741 GRT, built 1926) on passage from Tripoli to Trapani escorted by the Italian torpedo boat Generale Antonio Cascino. The Perla was sunk early on the 7th by aircraft torpedoes in position 215° - Pantelleria - 35 miles. (42)

7 Jan 1942 HMS Upright (Lt. J.S. Wraith, DSC, RN) patrolled in area 'L' at 70 feet owing to the rough seas which however moderated during the day. It was possible to keep periscope depth after 1400 B/7.
Between 1000 B/7 and 1020 B/7 a series of explosions had been heard. (41)
8 Jan 1942 HMS Upright (Lt. J.S. Wraith, DSC, RN) patrolled at 70 feet in area 'L'. The seas were again too rough keep a periscope depth patrol.
A leak was discovered in No.1 battery tank. A small handpump had to be mannded during the day. It was decided to abandon the patrol due to this leak and course was set to return to Malta.
In a signal timed 2010 B/8 the Capt. S.10 was informed of this. (41)
9 Jan 1942 Around 0830 B/9, HMS Upright (Lt. J.S. Wraith, DSC, RN) ended her 22nd war patrol (20th in the Mediterranean) at Malta. (41)
14 Jan 1942 HMS Upright (Lt. J.S. Wraith, DSC, RN) shoots down one German ME-109 fighter aircraft during an air raid at Malta. (44)
19 Jan 1942 Around 1900 B/19, HMS Upright (Lt. J.S. Wraith, DSC, RN) departed Malta for her 23rd war patrol (21st in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol off the north coast of Sicily.
22 Jan 1942 At 1210 B/22, HMS Upright (Lt. J.S. Wraith, DSC, RN) sighted a small coaster, around 800 tons, proceeding out of the Castellammare Gulf. The enemy was westbound.

At 1307 B/22, a signal timed 1153 B/22 was received in which the Capt. S.10 ordered HMS Upright to patrol in the vicinity of Cape Gallo to intercept a large 8000-ton transport ship which was expected to leave Palermo around dawn on 23 January.

An ULTRA signal had revealed that the German transport Wachftels (8467 GRT, 1928) escorted by the Italian torpedo boat Cigno and the gunboat (sic, torpedo boat) Procione were expected to leave Palermo at 0500 B/23 for Tripoli (see DEFE3/1/frame 498). Wachftels escorted only by Cigno actually sailed from Palermo at 0710 B/23 but had to stop at Pantelleria at 1900 B/23 as the torpedo boat had engine defects. They sailed from Pantelleria at 0430 B/24 and arrived at Trapani at 1200 B/24 and sailed again at 1700 B/24 and reached Palermo at 2320 B/24. It is not clear why Wachtfels also turned back and did not wait for the arrival of another escort. Eventually, Clio sailed again from Palermo at 1949 B/28 escorting the German transport Benghazi (former French Saint Philippe, 1567 GRT, built 1933) for Tripoli where they arrived at 0837 B/30 [perhaps the stores from Wachtfels were transferred to the Benghazi ?]. Wachtfels did not sail from Palermo for Tripoli until 22 February.
At 1945 B/22, HMS Upright surfaced and set course to patrol off Cape Gallo as ordered by the Capt. S.10. (41)
23 Jan 1942 At 1130 B/23, near Cape Gallo, HMS Upright (Lt. J.S. Wraith, DSC, RN) sighted three minesweepers rounding Cape Zaffarano and proceeding to Palermo.
We have been unable to identify there vessels.
At 1630 B/23, a trawler was seen to approach Palermo from the east.
At 1745 B/23, a small coastal steamer was seen to leave Palermo for the west. She kept very close to the coast.

At 2323 B/23, the Capt. S.10's signal timed 2155 B/23 was received in which he ordered HMS Upright to patrol in position 38.27'N, 15.32'E in anticipation of enemy heavy units proceeding northwards through the Straits of Messina.
Course was altered to the east to comply at 0001 B/24. (41)

24 Jan 1942 At 1235 B/24, north-north-west of the Straits of Messina, in approximate position 38°37'N, 15°27'E, HMS Upright (Lt. J.S. Wraith, DSC, RN) was passed by a small fishing trawler, which had a harpoon gun fitted. The enemy passed about a mile away on apposite course.
At 1340 B/24, a Generale-class torpedo boat was seen patrolling to the north of Messina.
This was most probably the Italian torpedo boat Giuseppe Cesare Abba which sailed from Messina at 0826 B/24 for listening exercises and returned at 1725 B/24.
At 1400 B/24, when in position 38°27'N, 15°32'E, an motor torpedo boat was seen about 2 miles away to the south.
At 1930 B/24, HMS Upright surfaced. During the night she patrolled about 8 miles to the north of Cape Rosocolmo. (41)

25 Jan 1942 At 0640 B/25, HMS Upright (Lt. J.S. Wraith, DSC, RN) dived 10 miles north of Cape Rosocolmo. Keeping periscope depth was impossible due to the heavy swell. HMS Upright only came to periscope depth for W/T routines. Nothing was heard or seen this day.
At 1950 B/25, HMS Upright surfaced. There was a north-west gale blowing. (41)

26 Jan 1942 At 0615 B/26, HMS Upright (Lt. J.S. Wraith, DSC, RN) dived 10 miles north of Cape Rosocolmo. Again keeping periscope depth was impossible due to the heavy swell and HMS Upright only came to periscope depth for W/T routines. Nothing was heard or seen this day.
At 1950 B/26, HMS Upright surfaced. The wind and sea eased down during the night. (41)

27 Jan 1942 At 0630 B/27, HMS Upright (Lt. J.S. Wraith, DSC, RN) dived in position 340° - Cape Rosocolmo - 11 miles. It was possible to keep a periscope patrol. No shipping was seen all day though. Many trains were seen to pass both ways along the coastal railway line, passing slowly over the long bridge over the Supondra River.
At 1945 B/27, HMS Upright surfaced. Course was set to return to the Palermo area.

28 Jan 1942 HMS Upright (Lt. J.S. Wraith, DSC, RN) patrolled north to north-east of Cape Zafferano. No shipping was sighted all day but numerous trains were observed on the coastal railway, both east- and westbound. (41)
29 Jan 1942 HMS Upright (Lt. J.S. Wraith, DSC, RN) patrolled off Cape Gallo all day. Again no shipping was sighted. The sea state was rough with a strong north-west wind.
At 2000 B/29, HMS Upright surfaced and left patrol for Malta. She was to pass 5 miles to the west of Marittimo during the night. (41)
1 Feb 1942 Around 0730 B/1, HMS Upright (Lt. J.S. Wraith, DSC, RN) ended her 23rd war patrol (21st in the Mediterranean) at Malta. (41)

9 Feb 1942 At 1940 B/9, HMS Upright (Lt. J.W.D. Coombe, RN) departed Malta for her 24th war patrol (22nd in the Mediterranean). She was ordered to patrol off the Gulf of Taranto. Spare Commanding Officer, Lt. Coombe had taken command vice Lt. Wraith for this patrol in order to give Lt. Wraith a rest.

She was to be part of a patrol line (3 - 39°25'N, 17°20'E - 60° - 10 - 150° - 20) together with HMS Una (Lt. D.S.R. Martin, RN) and HMS Tempest (Lt.Cdr. W.A.K.N. Cavaye, RN). HMS Una in position 'A' 39°25'N, 17°20'E, HMS Tempest in position 'B' and HMS Upright in position 'C'.

11 Feb 1942 At 0043 B/11, HMS Upright (Lt. J.W.D. Coombe, RN) was in position 37°29'N, 16°26'E when a submarine was sighted at only 300 yards on a course of 200°. Upright dived but could not regain contact. This was the Italian Malachite (T.V. Enzo Zanni) proceeding to a patrol off Cyrenaica, she also took avoiding action.
At 1900 B/11, on surfacing a submarine was sighted bearing 330° at a range of 4 miles. Course was the submarine was 030°. It was thought to be most probably HMS Una (Lt. D.S.R. Martin, RN). (41)
12 Feb 1942 At 1330 B/12, HMS Upright (Lt. J.W.D. Coombe, RN) sighted smoke bearing 260°. This smoke remained in sight all afternoon on a steady bearing but no ship was sighted.
At 1530 B/12, HMS Upright heard a distant explosion. This was HMS Una (Lt. D.S.R. Martin, RN) sinking an Italian tanker. (41)
13 Feb 1942 At 0335 B/13, HMS Upright (Lt. J.W.D. Coombe, RN) heard 10 distant depth charges.

At 0720 B/13, while dived, 7 heavy explosions were heard. Fairly close. From now on until 0955 B/13, there were 23 heavy explosions in all. It was considered that HMS Una (Lt. D.S.R. Martin, RN) was being hunted. This was however not the case as the victim was HMS Tempest (Lt.Cdr. W.A.K.N. Cavaye, RN).
At 1340 B/13, a distant explosion was heard.
At 1945 B/13, HMS Upright surfaced and patrolled on course 330° and 150°. (41)
14 Feb 1942 Between 0855 B/14 and 1422 B/14, HMS Upright (Lt. J.W.D. Coombe, RN) counted 17 distant explosions. Apparently the enemy were still hunting in the general area. (41)
16 Feb 1942 At 0100 B/16, HMS Upright (Lt. J.W.D. Coombe, RN) received the Capt. S.10's signal timed 1110 B/15, ordering her to patrol near Alice Point. Course was altered accordingly.
Between 1012 B/16 and 1141 B/16, 12 distant explosions were heard.
